We obtain high signal-to-noise spectra at six pointings along the major axis of M31 out to 700'' (2.7 kpc ) and a single pointing of the central 10 pc for M32. In M31 the sodium NaI 8190 index shows a flat equivalent width profile at 0.4 through the majority of the bulge, with a strong gradient up to 0.8 in the central 10'' (38 pc ) ; the Wing-Ford FeH 9916 index is measured to be constant at 0.4 for all radii; and calcium triplet CaT 8498, 8542, 8662 shows a gradual increase through the bulge towards the centre.

We find that M31 is well described on all scales by a Chabrier IMF, with a gradient in sodium enhancement of [ Na/Fe ] +0.3 dex in the outer bulge, rising within the central 10'' to perhaps [ Na/Fe ] +1.0 dex in the nuclear region.

sources.summary: Andromeda is modelled with the Milky Way in the Local Group timing argument; including the LMC lowers the inferred pair mass and changes the predicted encounter geometry.

quotes.quote: We show that accounting for the presence of the LMC systematically lowers the Local Group mass ( M_ ) derived from the relative motion of the Milky Way--Andromeda pair. The masses of the Milky Way, M_ =1.04_ 0.23 ^ +0.26 10^ 12 M_ , and Andromeda, M_ =1.33_ 0.33 ^ +0.39 10^ 12 M_ , are consistent with previous estimates that neglect the impact of the LMC on the observed Hubble flow.

Models that neglect the LMC mass, f_c 0 , recover the main results found by van der Marel Guhathakurta (2012a), that is a LG mass, M 4.5 10^ 12 M_ , and an orbital pericentre r_ peri 20 , which imply that our Galaxy and M31 will suffer a head-on encounter 4 from now (van der Marel et al. 2012b). In particular, for f_c 0.2 the LG mass drops down to M 2.8 10^ 12 M_ , in better agreement with the values independently derived by D14, M=(2.5 0.4) 10^ 12 M_ , and P14, M=(2.3 0.7) 10^ 12 M_ , while the pericentric distance increases up to r_ peri 100 , indicating a fly-by encounter rather than a head-on collision.

sources.summary: The CoDa simulation presents an M31 progenitor cutout whose gas lies in sheets, filaments, and knots; these structures grow while small-scale structure is smoothed by merging and UV heating.

quotes.quote: Maps: Zoom-In on Galaxies and the Cosmic Web during the EoR Example: The M31 Progenitor Cut-out The physical properties of the gas for the M31 progenitor cutout at 3 different epochs are shown in Fig. f:maps . The gas density and ionizing flux density are averaged over a 625 thick slice, while the temperature and neutral fraction shown are taken from a 1 cell thick slice (i.e. 15.625 ). The gas is distributed along sheets, filaments and knots, which become more pronounced with time. The small scale structure visible in the high redshift maps progressively disappears, due to a combination of hierarchical merging and smoothing due to heating by UV radiation.

sources.summary: The paper describes Andromeda as hosting a thin rotating great plane of satellites, correlated with the Milky Way satellite structure and central to Local Group anisotropy arguments.

quotes.quote: Andromeda has a very thin great-plane of Andromeda (GPoA) made up of half of all its satellite galaxies (Metz et al. 2007; Metz et al. 2009; Ibata et al. 2013, 2014a). Both the DoS/VPOS and the GPoA are rotational structures. Furthermore (Pawlowski et al. 2013): (i) Not only are the VPOS and the GPoA extremely pronounced and incompatible with the dark-matter models, they are also mutually correlated in that the GPoA points precisely at the Milky Way (MW) and both the VPOS and the GPoA have spin vectors which point into a similar direction. (ii) The entire Local Group has a highly symmetrical structure around the Milky-Way--Andromeda axis which is, by all counts of probability and by virtue of the thinness of the non-satellite vast planar structures, impossible to obtain in structure formation within the SMoC, in which the dwarf galaxies in the Local Group have largely independent formation and infall histories.

sources.summary: The paper uses M31 as the observational benchmark for rotating outer-halo globular clusters, comparing its measured rotation signal and stellar halo substructure with Aquarius mock halos.

quotes.quote: Motivated by the detection of a significant rotation signal in the outer halo GC system of M31, we investigate the likelihood of detecting such a rotation signal in projection, using cosmological simulations.

The ability to detect this rotation signal is highly dependent on the viewing perspective, and the probability of seeing a signal larger than that measured in M31 ranges from 10 to 90 for the different halos in the Aquarius suite.

Figure f:M31vsAq presents important similarities between M31's stellar halo, shown in the left panel, and that of Aq-C for instance, displayed in the right panel. Both halos are abundant in stellar substructures of various forms that are the indications of past accretion events.

sources.summary: The paper reviews SPLASH results on Andromeda, including resolved stellar populations, halo substructure, a power-law surface-brightness profile, metallicity gradient, and heated disk stars.

quotes.quote: Large scale surveys of Andromeda's resolved stellar populations have revolutionized our view of this galaxy over the past decade. The combination of large-scale, contiguous photometric surveys and pointed spectroscopic surveys has been particularly powerful for discovering substructure and disentangling the structural components of Andromeda. The SPLASH (Spectroscopic and Photometric Landscape of Andromeda's Stellar Halo) survey consists of broad- and narrow-band imaging and spectroscopy of red giant branch stars in lines of sight ranging in distance from 2 kpc to more than 200 kpc from Andromeda's center. The SPLASH data reveal a power-law surface brightness profile extending to at least two-thirds of Andromeda's virial radius ( ), a metallicity gradient extending to at least 100 kpc from Andromeda's center ( ), and evidence of a significant population of heated disk stars in Andromeda's inner halo ( ).

sources.summary: The paper searches Fermi-LAT data for a gamma-ray halo around M31 and reports a diffuse halo whose bubble-like morphology suggests past nuclear or star-formation activity.

quotes.quote: We have searched for such a halo around the nearby giant spiral Andromeda galaxy M31 using almost 7 years of Fermi LAT data at energies above 300 MeV. The presence of a diffuse gamma-ray halo with total photon flux 2.6 0.6 10^ 9 cm ^ 2  s ^ 1 , corresponding to a luminosity (0.3-100 GeV) of (3.2 0.6) 10^ 38 erg s ^ 1 (for a distance of 780 kpc) was found at a 5.3 confidence level. The halo form does not correspond to the extended baryonic HI disc of M31, as would be expected in hadronic production of gamma photons from cosmic ray interaction, nor it is spherically symmetric, as could be in the case of dark matter annihilation. The best-fit halo template corresponds to two 6-7.5 kpc bubbles symmetrically located perpendicular to the M31 galactic disc, similar to the 'Fermi bubbles' found around the Milky Way centre, which suggests the past activity of the central supermassive black hole or a star-formation burst in M31. gamma rays: galaxies, galaxies: individual:M31, ISM: magnetic fields, cosmic rays Extended baryonic haloes around spiral galaxies can exist due to gas inflow from their neighbourhood White1978,Fukugita2006 .

sources.summary: The paper describes Z-PAndAS mapping of M31's disk, halo, satellites, and streams, then uses dwarf-satellite and Giant Southern Stream work to study M31's evolution and mass profile.

quotes.quote: As part of the Z-PAndAS Keck II DEIMOS survey of resolved stars in our neighboring galaxy, Andromeda (M31), we have built up a unique data set of measured velocities and chemistries for thousands of stars in the Andromeda stellar halo, particularly probing its rich and complex substructure. In this contribution, we will discuss the structural, dynamical and chemical properties of Andromeda's dwarf spheroidal galaxies, and how there is no observational evidence for a difference in the evolutionary histories of those found on and off M31's vast plane of satellites. We will also discuss a possible extension to the most significant merger event in M31 - the Giant Southern Stream - and how we can use this feature to refine our understanding of M31's mass profile, and its complex evolution.

This follow-up survey - named Z-PAndAS - has been used to measure the masses of M31's vast satellite system (e.g. ), uncover an extended and thick disk population in the system ( ), map the M31 halo ( ) and analyse a number of its streams and substructures (e.g., ).

sources.summary: The paper describes M31 as a massive early-type Local Group spiral with an anemic star-formation rate, a CO-bright 10 kpc ring, and molecular gas whose inferred boundedness changes with scale.

quotes.quote: M31 is a massive early-type spiral also in the Local Group. It has fairly anemic star formation e.g., see LEWIS15 and a large stellar bulge. The CARMA map by Schruba et al. targets mostly the 10 kpc molecular ring, which hosts most of the CO emission in the galaxy NIETEN06 ; however the southwest field covers part of M31's bulge.
The bottom left panel of Figure fig:multiscale shows that _ vir ^ 1 rises significantly moving from =60 pc to =30 pc, increasing by factors of 3 for M31 and 2 for the LMC.

sources.summary: The paper describes M31's outer halo as being built by accreting dwarf satellites and uses globular-cluster abundances, kinematics, and stream associations to probe that assembly.

quotes.quote: Observations of stellar streams in M31's outer halo suggest that M31 is actively accreting several dwarf galaxies and their globular clusters (GCs). Detailed abundances can chemically link clusters to their birth environments, establishing whether or not a GC has been accreted from a satellite dwarf galaxy. This talk presents the detailed chemical abundances of seven M31 outer halo GCs (with projected distances from M31 greater than 30 kpc), as derived from high-resolution integrated-light spectra taken with the Hobby Eberly Telescope. Five of these clusters were recently discovered in the Pan-Andromeda Archaeological Survey (PAndAS)---this talk presents the first determinations of integrated Fe, Na, Mg, Ca, Ti, Ni, Ba, and Eu abundances for these clusters.

The abundances of PA53 and PA56 indicate that the progenitor of the Eastern Cloud was likely similar to the Fornax dwarf galaxy. Together, the combination of resolved photometry and IL spectroscopy has demonstrated that M31's outer halo is at least partially been built up by the accretion of satellite dwarfs. Studies of the outer halo field stars and the GCs have provided essential information about the masses of the satellites that are being accreted to form M31's outer halo. This level of detail will be difficult to achieve in more distant galaxies, particularly when the field stars cannot be resolved.

sources.summary: The paper builds an M31 benchmark model with a dark halo, stellar bulge, and exponential disk to test GOTHIC performance on a realistic particle distribution.

quotes.quote: In order to measure the performance in a realistic distribution, we generate a model of the Andromeda galaxy ( M31 ). The mass distribution model of M31 is given by Geehan2006, Fardal2007 . Its composition is a dark matter halo with an NFW profile (the mass is 8.11 10^ 11 M_ and the scale length is 7.63 kpc) with 7,730,866 particles, a stellar bulge with a Hernquist profile (the mass is 3.24 10^ 10 M_ and the scale radius is 0.61 kpc) with 308,853 particles, and an exponential disk (the mass is 3.66 10^ 10 M_ , the scale length is 5.4 kpc, and the scale height is 0.6 kpc) with 348,889 particles. The total number of N body particles is 2^ 23 = 8,388,608, the masses of all N body particles are identical and the Plummer softening length is set to 16 pc.

sources.summary: The paper treats M31/Andromeda as one of the two dominant Local Group spiral galaxies, emphasizing uncertain dark-matter halo mass estimates and assuming M31 is the more massive member in simulations.

quotes.quote: In particular, the exact size of the two halos hosting the Milky Way (MW) and Andromeda (M31) galaxies is known only within a large degree of uncertainty. Viral mass estimates for the DM haloes of both M31 and MW are still just loosely constrained by observations Kochanek:1996, Wilkinson:1999, Evans:2000, Marel:2008, Karachentsev:2009, Marel:2012, Gibbons:2014 and in general need a substantial degree of modelling and theory to be inferred see e.g. Peebles:2001, Li:2008, McMillian:2011, Fardal:2013, Boylan-Kolchin:2013, Diaz:2014, Cautun:2014, Wang:2015 .

Following the observation that the LG system is dominated by a pair of spiral galaxies, the MW and M31, simulated LGs are first of all defined as a pair of DM haloes. M31 (MW) is assumed to be the most (least) massive of the two, as suggested by a host of different considerations such as the measure of the tidal force acting on the MW Baiesi:2009 , the position of the barycenter of the LG given by the local Hubble flow Karachentsev:2009 and the balance of the total angular momentum at the center of mass Diaz:2014 .

sources.summary: The review describes M31 as the best local laboratory for mapping multi-phase CGM absorbers with multiple sight lines, including AMIGA HST/COS observations behind the galaxy.

quotes.quote: The best local laboratory to carry out such an experiment is M31. recently analyzed 18 sight lines within 2 R_ vir 600 kpc of M31, thus producing a partial map of the multi-phase CGM around the galaxy. New HST/COS observations of quasars behind M31 as part of the AMIGA (Absorption Maps In the Gas of Andromeda) project (PI N. Lehner) will improve on this pilot analysis with a total of 25 sight lines within 1.1R_ vir . While these observations will not constrain structure on the fine scales possible with gravitationally lensed quasars or photoionization modeling, the spatially resolved map of M31's CGM will help us better interpret observations of single sight lines through the halos of similar-mass galaxies, such as those from the COS-Halos program 2011Sci...334..948T .

sources.summary: The paper presents an extensive Spitzer point-source catalog for M 31, covering its disk, comparing its CMD to other galaxies, and highlighting its resolved mid-IR populations.

quotes.quote: We present Spitzer IRAC 3.6-8 and MIPS 24 point-source catalogs for M 31 and 15 other mostly large, star forming galaxies at distances 3.5-14 Mpc including M 51 , M 83 , M 101 and NGC 6946 . These catalogs contain 1 million sources including 859,000 in M 31 and 116,000 in the other galaxies.
In this paper, we present an extensive mid-IR point-source catalog of M 31 consisting of 859,000 sources, covering the entirety of M 31 's disk including the accompanying M 32 and M 110 galaxies.

The blue-ward extent of the M 31 CMD is consistent with, e.g, the comparable CMDs of M 33 shown on Fig. 14 of ref:McQuinn_2007 and Fig. 4 of ref:Khan_2015b , and it contains a larger fraction of blue sources than the 15 galaxies beyond the local group cataloged here.

sources.summary: The paper identifies M31 as an individually detected nearby ray source, removes it from the point-source SFG stack because it is extended, and notes its FIR luminosity is atypically low for the sample.

quotes.quote: While the majority of galaxies in our universe produce a ray flux far below the sensitivity of current instruments, a handful of galaxies have been individually detected. These include several of our closest neighbors (LMC, SMC and M31), as well as some of the most intense nearby star-forming galaxies (NGC 1068, NGC 3034 (M 82), NGC 4945, and Arp 220) Acero:2015hja, Peng:2016nsx .

Second, we have removed the LMC, SMC and M31 from this sample, as they are extended ray sources that may not be accurately modeled with the point-source analysis technique employed here. Additionally, the FIR luminosities for these systems are significantly smaller than the typical galaxies in our analysis. Detailed ray analyses of these galaxies exist in the literature 2010A&A...523L...2A, 2015PhRvD..91j2001B, 2015arXiv150103460P, 2016PhRvD..93f2004C , and we will compare our results to these systems in the discussion.

sources.summary: The paper uses Andromeda galaxy as a comparison for its Milky Way density method, noting an earlier model, a valid E_z approximation only to z 0.8 kpc, and a non-maximum-disc mass model.

quotes.quote: We checked validity of this assumption in case of the Andromeda galaxy, M 31, modelled by us earlier Kipper:2016 . In case of the Andromeda galaxy at R = 8 kpc the third integral can be approximated as E_z up to z 0.8 kpc only.

Comparing this to a similar Andromeda galaxy mass distribution model Tamm:2012 , the local mass density at the distance of R = 8 kpc , we can notice a difference. Although the local density value in M 31 is quite similar, the best model of M 31 is not a maximum disc model and dark matter at R = 8 kpc gives a quite noticeable contribution to the total density value.

sources.summary: The paper models an MW-M 31 MOND encounter where transferred MW material builds M 31 tidal clouds, a baryonic halo-scale cloud, and a rotating planar structure resembling the GPoA.

quotes.quote: The simulated encounter led to the transfer of 3 of the MW mass to M 31 along a tidal tail ( res , simall ()).
The encounter formed clouds of particles around the simulated MW and M 31. The mass of the cloud formed at the simulated M 31 was close to the real baryonic halo mass of M 31.

At the time when the simulation reproduced the observed MW-M 31 separation and relative velocity, the matter transferred to the simulated M 31 was forming a planar structure resembling the GPoA (Figs. fig:m31sun and fig:m31sp ) by size, by being oriented by its edge toward the simulated MW and by the same sense of rotation.

sources.summary: The paper compares simulations to the observed M31 satellite system, finding its satellite distribution more spherical and typical of LCDM than the Milky Way's highly atypical planar distribution.

quotes.quote: The most oustanding feature of this plot is that for w and /a ratio the MW is always two standard deviations away from the mean, while M31 is always within 2 standard deviations. The results for the b/a ratio are always 2 standard deviations both for the MW and M31. This confirms the extreme planar distribution for the MW and the more spherical distribution for the M31.

While M31 presents a fully typical asphericity in the expectations from LCDM, the MW shows aspherical deviations in plane width and the major-to-minor axis ratio highly atypical in the framework of LCDM, confirming the original hint by and more recent results 2012MNRAS.423.1109P,2015ApJ...815...19P .

sources.summary: The paper compares M31 globular clusters with M85, using ULySS ages and metallicities and noting both systems contain intermediate-age, relatively metal-rich clusters unlike the mostly old Milky Way sample.

quotes.quote: (1) hwa08 , (2) cot01 , (3) cot03 , (4) ric04 , (5) pff04 , (6) sch06 , (7) lee10 , (8) rom09 , (9) pot15 , (10) pot13 , (11) csb08 In Figure fig:measure_dist2 , we compare the ages and metallicities of the M85 GCs with those of the Milky Way GCs and M31 GCs that were derived from the full spectrum fitting with ULySS. We used the ages and metallicities of the Milky Way GCs and M31 GCs given by cez13 . We note that the GCs in M31 and M85 consist of not only old GCs but also intermediate-age GCs (with age < 8 Gyr), while the GCs in the Milky Way Galaxy are predominantly old ( > 10 Gyr). The intermediate-age GCs in M31 and M85 are, on average, more metal-rich than the old GCs in the same galaxies, which is consistent with the results for the GCs in other early-type galaxies puz05, pie06a, par12 .

sources.summary: The paper frames M31 as the Milky Way's Local Group partner, citing flattened and highly lopsided satellite distributions around M31 and a prior result finding no M31 satellite shape-alignment signal.

quotes.quote: Regardless, the case of the satellite shape alignment in galaxy pairs still aroused our interest because our Local Group is dominated by a pair of primary galaxies: Milky Way and M31. A flattening of satellites distribution is found empirically in the Milky Way e.g. 1976MNRAS.174..695L, 2005A&A...431..517K, 2012MNRAS.423.1109P and in M31 2013ApJ...766..120C, 2013Natur.493...62I, 2013MNRAS.435.1928P , which is not an obvious prediction of generic galaxy formation models in the CDM paradigm.

Therefore, the distribution of satellite in galaxy pairs is gaining increasing attention owing to the observational fact that the satellite galaxy system of M31 is highly lopsided, with about 80 per cent of its satellites lying on the side facing the Milky Way 2013ApJ...766..120C .
provide evidence to show that the major axes of the Milky Way satellites lie within a preferential plane, while no signal was found in M31.

sources.summary: The paper treats M31 as a key nearby target for X-ray dark-matter decay tests, predicting a baryon-sensitive radial flux profile from simulated Local Group analogues.

quotes.quote: The M31 galaxy is of particular interest to X-ray decay studies due to its extent on the sky: we can take pointings at multiple radii to examine whether the measured signal is well described by a dark matter profile as would be the case for a dark matter decay line, or instead by a profile that traces the gas and thus disfavours a dark matter interpretation. The small scales probed by these observations in such a nearby object, of the order of parsecs, imply that measurements are sensitive to the effect of baryons on the dark matter halo as illustrated in Fig. MvXDMO . We consider four pointings, at displacements from the centre of M31 of 0.0', 8.3', 25.0' and 60.0' made for a distance to M31 of 750 kpc McConnachie05 .
We conclude that predictions for the M31 radial flux profile are sensitive to baryon physics, and are steeper than predicted by the NFW profile.

sources.summary: The paper discusses M31 halo substructure, its spatial association with globular clusters, South West Cloud cluster ages, and stream-linked globular-cluster populations.

quotes.quote: In M31, there is a striking spatial correlation between stellar substructure and GCs beyond 30 from the galactic centre Mackey2010 . It was concluded that there is a less than 1 chance that these GCs are in their spatial configuration by chance Mackey2010,Veljanoski2014 and are therefore likely to have been accreted with the stars comprising the substructure.
estimate that 2 of the GCs (PA-7 and PA-8) associated with the M31 substructure known as the South West Cloud have ages of 6-10 , which makes them at least 3 younger than the oldest MW GCs.

Observations of the GC population outside 30 of the centre of M31 have shown that a large fraction of these GCs are situated on streams Mackey2010 and this has also been found to be the case for other galaxies outside the Local Group (e.g.

sources.summary: The paper treats M31 as a Local Group host whose satellite stellar mass-metallicity relation follows the same universal MZR found for Milky Way satellites and comparable halo-mass systems.

quotes.quote: M31 is another big galaxy in the Local Group, and the host halo mass of M31 is also possibly in the range 1 - 2 10^ 12 (e.g., Kafleetal18 and references therein). The results shown in Figures fig:mw100 and fig:sats_full support that the MZR of the satellites in both M31 and MW follows a universal MZR.

Our study shows that the slope of the MZR for the satellites in a halo with mass the same as MW/M31 halo mass ( [ Fe/H ] - M_* ) are mostly in the same range of 0.2--0.4 (i.e., with a scatter of 0.2 for different halo assembly histories), and the intercept at (M_*/ )=3 is b 2.5 to 2.9 . If the satellites of many halos are stacked together, the MZR for the stacked satellites in MW/M31-size halos gives 0.35 0.03 and the intercept b 2.7 0.1 .

sources.summary: The paper uses dense Subaru/HSC monitoring of M31 stars to evaluate PBH microlensing, with one possible event and strong constraints tied to Milky Way and M31 halo dark matter.

quotes.quote: Niikuraetal_PBH:17 , where authors of this paper are co-authors, used the new wide-field prime-focus camera at the 8.2m Subaru telescope, Hyper Suprime-Cam (HSC), to carry out very dense cadence observation (2 min cadence) of the Andromeda galaxy (hereafter M31). Thanks to the wide field-of-view and large aperture of HSC/Subaru, they were able to monitor many stars in M31 (about 770 kpc in distance or 24.4 mag for the distance modulus) and to search for microlensing events of much shorter timescales than previously done. They found one possible PBH microlensing event compared to the theoretically-expected number of events up to 1000 events if PBHs make up all DM in the Milky Way and M31 halo regions. The results were then translated into most stringent upper bounds on the abundance of PBHs over the range of mass scales, M_ PBH 10^ 11 ,10^ 7 M_ .

sources.summary: For M31, the paper reports B[e] supergiants found among LBV candidates, thirteen proposed objects, and a revised assessment leaving four confirmed, seven uncertain, and two misclassified.

quotes.quote: To date, systematic spectroscopic studies have been performed in the two large spiral galaxies of the Local Group, M31 and M33, in which a number of B [ e ] SGs were found amongst the putative LBV candidates ( ).
M31 A total of 13 B [ e ] SGs has been proposed in M31 to date.

However, a closer look at this total sample of 13 objects, including the available information from near- and mid-IR photometric observations, reveals that only four stars can be considered as confirmed B [ e ] SGs. Seven objects require further clarifications, and two appear to be misclassified (see Table tab:M31 ).

sources.summary: The paper treats Andromeda as a separate gravitational contributor to relic-neutrino overdensity, modeling it with a spherical NFW profile and evolving dark-matter component, while finding that including Andromeda has little effect.

quotes.quote: We review the calculation of the local overdensity of relic neutrinos produced from the gravitational attraction of our Galaxy, Andromeda and the Virgo cluster, commenting on the implications for an experiment aiming at relic neutrino detection, such as the PTOLEMY project.

For the Andromeda galaxy and the Virgo cluster, accounted for in together with the Milky Way, we only consider a spherical NFW profile, neglecting the baryonic content of Andromeda. The redshift evolution of the DM components of the Milky Way, Andromeda and Virgo is taken into account following the N body trend from , with a fixed virial mass and evolving virial concentration and radius.
Including Andromeda does not affect much n_ , , but the Virgo cluster has a non-negligible effect (see Fig. fig:resumen-NFW ) that is more pronounced the smaller the value of m_ .

sources.summary: The review describes M31 extinction maps and measurements, noting a steeper-than-Galactic bulge curve, a disk curve similar to the Galactic mean, and possible shared Milky Way-M31 extinction trends.

quotes.quote: In the local Universe, much of our understanding of extinction curves outside of the Galaxy or Clouds comes from HST enabled dust extinction maps in M31 that were derived using novel color-magnitude diagram fitting methods Dalcanton2015,Dong2016 . Dong2014 measured the extinction curve toward the bulge of M31, and find a curve steeper than the Galactic average ( R_ V 2.4 -2.5). Of particular note is that HST observations of the disk in M31 reveal an extinction curve with comparable slope to the Galactic mean, though with possibly a weaker bump Bianchi1996 . These results, taken together, hint at common trends in the extinction curve in the Milky Way and M31.

sources.summary: The paper cites M31 as a contrasting globular-cluster system where MDF bimodality is debated, M31 metallicities drive a CZR break point, and metal-poor M31 clusters help reveal non-linear CZRs.

quotes.quote: However, in some galaxies such as M31, the bimodality of the GC MDF is debated (e.g. Barmby2000, Galleti2009 ), with recent studies indicating even a trimodal distribution Caldwell2016 .

The position of the break point is strongly driven by the metallicities of M31 GCs Caldwell2011 because the other galaxies in this collection show no GC metallicities < -1.2 dex. In the sample, M31 is also the only LTG, while the others are massive ETGs.
Non-linear relations are predominantly found when incorporating measurements of metal-poor GCs, for example, from the MW or M31.

sources.summary: The paper uses M31 as a well-surveyed host with classical satellites complete to 300 kpc and 3D satellite positions for projected-profile comparisons.

quotes.quote: The M31 satellites come from mcconnachie2018 with distances primarily from weisz2019 . In brief, we assume that the MW and M31 systems are complete to classical satellites ( M_V 8 ) within the inner 300 kpc.

For the MW and M31, we have detailed 3D positions for the satellites, and we use that to mock `re-observe' these systems at a distance of 7 Mpc. A few of the most massive hosts (M81, CenA, and M31) have spatial distributions characteristic of the simulated hosts, but the majority of the observed hosts are more concentrated, and, most importantly, none are less concentrated.

sources.summary: The paper uses M31 as an observational metal-retention comparison, citing PHAT-based work that found 62 percent of metal mass lost from its central 19 kpc.

quotes.quote: telford19 analysed the third of M31 covered by the Panchromatic Hubble Andromeda Treasury PHAT, dalcanton12 , finding that 62 per cent of all metal mass has been lost from the central 19 kpc of the galaxy.
For NGC 628 and M31, oxygen loss fractions are given within 7 kpc and 19 kpc, respectively, whereas the model of peeples14 considers all material within 150 kpc of a galaxy.

We also show observationally derived values for NGC 628 blue star; belfiore16 and M31 green square; telford19 . It is clear that both the measurements for individual galaxies and the model region of peeples14 lie significantly above our simulated galaxies, indicating a larger fraction of oxygen has been lost than our model predicts.

sources.summary: The paper treats Andromeda galaxy's eccentric nuclear stellar disc as a possible rotating, lopsided thermodynamic equilibrium, noting its old stellar content and proposing future fits with these equilibria.

quotes.quote: Eccentric stellar disc in the nucleus of Andromeda galaxy may be an example of a lopsided thermodynamic equilibrium of a rotating black hole star cluster.

The nuclear cluster in Andromeda has similar structure which led to model it as an ``eccentric disc''. The disc consists of old stars stars 2005ApJ...631..280B and is likely dynamically old, so one may expect it to reach secular-dynamical equilibrium. It would therefore be of interest to fit the data in Andromeda using rotating lopsided equilibria that we are finding; this is a subject for future work.

sources.summary: The paper uses M31 as the dynamical reference galaxy, discussing its tangential motion, velocity zero point, and virial mass needed to bind NGC 147 and NGC 185.

quotes.quote: The Galactocentric velocity of M31 ( v _ M31,GC ) in Equation e:vtransform is essential since it provides the zero point of the satellites' motions. The line-of-sight velocity ( ) of M31 has been known with great accuracy since the first measurement by sli13 , but direct measurements of tangential motion has only been available since 2012. Using multi-epoch \ imaging data obtained using ACS/WFC and WFC3/UVIS, soh12 determined absolute PM of M31 stars in three fields.
Using escape velocities based on the measured 3-d velocities, we find that M31 must have a minimal virial mass of 0.5-1 10^ 12 for both NGC 147 and NGC 185 to be bound to M31's halo simultaneously.

sources.summary: The paper treats M31/Andromeda as a main Local Group component and uses likelihood-free inference with its distance and velocity to estimate the Milky Way-plus-M31 mass at 4.6 10^12 M_200.

quotes.quote: In this work we perform a LFI-based parameter estimation of the sum of masses of the Milky Way and M31.
The Milky Way and Andromeda are the main components of the Local Group, which includes tens of smaller galaxies.

In this work we have used Density Estimation Likelihood-Free Inference with forward-modelling to estimate the posterior distribution for sum of the masses of the Milky Way and M31 using observations of the distance and velocity to M31. We obtain a mass M_ MW+M31 = parse-numbers=false 4.6^ +2.3 _ 1.8 10^ 12 ( M_ 200 ).

sources.summary: The paper treats M31 as a literature comparison and outlier in the feedback-time versus stellar-mass relation, possibly because only its outer 6-13 kpc field was analyzed.

quotes.quote: In the third panel, we only add M31 and M33 from the literature compilation to ensure that the GMC surface density measurements are homogeneous, because the other literature galaxies are not included by Sun2018 .

There is only one clear outlier, M31, with a mass just over 10^ 11 . It is unclear if this galaxy is a true outlier (possibly linked to the fact that the analysed field of view covers only the outer parts of the galaxy, between 6 and 13 kpc), or if the relation between the feedback time-scale and the stellar mass breaks above M_ 10^ 10.5 , similarly to the cosmic baryon efficiency Moster2013, Behroozi2013, Moster2018, Behroozi2019 .

While we cannot test this idea definitively given that we presently only have a measurement for M31 at stellar masses M_ >10^ 10.75 , it will be straightforward to investigate this further once the full PHANGS-ALMA sample has been analysed with our statistical method.

sources.summary: The paper uses new high-spectral-resolution VLA observations of M31 and M33 to test whether H I line shapes imply opaque gas or multiple line-of-sight components.

quotes.quote: Given these open questions, we investigate the topic of opacity using new observations of M31 and M33 with 0.42   spectral resolution taken with the Karl G. Jansky Very Large Array (VLA). In particular, we address the question: do the observed line shapes in M31 and M33 imply a single opaque component, as suggested by Braun2009ApJ...695..937B , or are the spectra better described by multiple components along the line-of-sight? Answering this question has broad implications for our understanding of the neutral ISM in the nearby universe, since the ISM mass is dominated by the atomic phase SaintongeCatinella2017ApJS..233...22S,WalterCarilli2020arXiv200911126W , and constraints on the cosmic density of in galaxies at z=0 depend on our ability to accurately measure the atomic gas mass.
In sec:mod_comp , we use the model comparison and present our main result: most spectra in M31 and M33 are best-modelled with a multi-component Gaussian model.

sources.summary: The paper treats M31 as a star-forming galaxy gamma-ray source, uses a power-law spectral model, notes its hard spectrum, and interprets its low cutoff energy as particle evolution.

quotes.quote: Owing to a small data sets, we selected a power-law (PL) model as the spectral model of M31 and M33, based on Feng2019 and Xi2020 . For all SBGs, we chose the PL spectral model; for those of SMC and LMC, we selected the log parabola (LOG) model by referring to 4FGL. Using the binned maximum likelihood method, we fitted the photon events of all 4FGL sources within the 30 ^ range around each target source. Here, the two background templates, including Galactic ( gll iem v07.fits ) and extragalactic diffuse emissions ( iso P8R3 SOURCE V2 V1.txt ) http://fermi.gsfc.nasa.gov/ssc/data/access/lat/BackgroundModels.html , were added to each source model file, where their normalizations were set as free parameters. Within 5^ of the center of the ROI, the normalization and spectral index of each source were set as free parameters as well. Spectral Energy Distribution To derive the spectral energy distribution (SED) of each source, the energy range from 100 MeV to 500 GeV was divided into six equally spaced log10( E ) energy bins for Circinus, NGC 2146, NGC 3424, M31; for Arp 299 and M 33, we generated five energy bins for a low data statistic, referring to Xi2020 . For the SEDs of other sources, we chose to generate 10 energy bins. Because the statistics and TS values from the last three bins were less for NGC 253 and M82, we considered combining the three bins to one, based on Abdalla2018 . For each bin of all SEDs, we guaranteed that their TS values were greater than 4. For the energy bin with a TS value < 4, the upper limits of the 95 confidence level were given. All SEDs of each source are shown in Figure Fig1 . The global fit results for each source are given in Table Tab1 . We found that the average value of the power-law spectral index of SBGs is approximately 2.23, which is consistent with the maximum likelihood analysis results of Ackermann2012a and Ajello2020 . For each panel of Figure Fig1 , we provided the differential sensitivities of Large High Altitude Air Shower Observatory Bai2019 and the Cherenkov Telescope Array in the northern/southern hemisphere CTA2019 , to predict their likely very-high-energy (VHE) emission in the future. 1.5 Model Introduction The tool, NAIMA , provided a one-zone stationary model and the proton-proton (pp) interaction differential cross-section of PYTHIA 8 Kafexhiu2014 to fit the spectra of the target sources. This tool employs the Markov chain Monte Carlo method, implemented in the emcee package Foreman2013 . We will use NAIMA to complete all the following analyses. The high-energy radiations in SFGs and SBGs are thought to be caused by CRs colliding with the surrounding interstellar medium (ISM) resulting in the generation of neutral pions and other products Abdo2010b,Abdo2010d,Peretti2019 . In addition, the neutral pions can decay into high-energy ray photons Stecker1971,Dermer1986 ; thus, the SBGs and SFGs were considered to be possible ray sources in the local universe Dermer1986,Strong1976,Paglione1996,Blom1999,Domingo2005 . The ray radiation of SBGs and SFGs above 100 MeV is widely regarded to be of hadronic origin; whereas pionic decay is regarded as the dominant mechanism of ray generation inside them e.g., Domingo2005,Rephaeli2010,Tang2014,Abdalla2018,Peretti2019,Wang2018 . Therefore, we assumed that the high-energy radiation of SBGs and SFGs originated from the collective interaction of internal SNRs and molecular cloud gas inside those starburst regions, and that pionic decay is the dominant radiation mechanism for the GeV ray emission of the SBGs and SFGs. For the absorption of extragalactic background light (EBL), here we consider the EBL model from Dominguez2011 in all the following analyses. For SNRs in the Milky Way, the protonic energy distribution of a power-law distribution with an exponential cutoff (ECPL) has been widely used to explain their ray spectra Aharonian2006,Xing2016, Xin2019,Xiang2021a . Here, we assumed that the protonic energy distributions of SNRs within SBGs and SFGs satisfy the same formula, which is as follows: N(E) = N_ 0 ( E E_ 0 )^ exp (- E E_ cutoff , where N_ 0 is the amplitude, E is the particle energy, is the power-law spectral index, and E_ cutoff is the cutoff energy, and E_ 0 = 1 TeV. Model fit Diffusive shock acceleration (DSA), as a predominant acceleration mechanism, is generally used to explain high-energy particle acceleration at SNR shocks of up to approximately 100 TeV or even higher Aharonian2007,Aharonian2011,Morlino2012a . Therefore, here the range of protonic energy was selected from 0.1 GeV to 0.5 PeV e.g., Abdalla2018 . When the protonic energy distributions of the SBGs and SFGs satisfy (1), we used NAIMA to obtain the best-fit results of the SEDs of all sources, as shown in Figure Fig2 . The related best-fit parameters are given in Table Tab2 , where the protons energy budget W_ p was calculated to be above 290 MeV, which is considered as the pion production threshold Abdalla2018 . Except for the hardening spectral components from NGC 1068 and NGC 4945, we found that the one-zone model with ECPL can explain the SEDs of most SBGs and SFGs. However, we found that the first bins of SEDs of NGC 1068, M82, NGC 253, Arp 299, and SMC cannot be well explained; we assumed that the first bins may be from the contributions of the Bremsstrahlung emission and inverse Compton scattering of the primary or secondary electrons of CRs e.g., Tang2014 . h 1.2 Note: The last column is the references of distance, gas density, and redshift of each source, respectively. The column of N_ SNR is SNR's number inside each source. Here we assumed that the gas density of NGC 3424 was 10 cm ^ 3 since its spectrum and photon flux of the global fit were similar to those of NGC 2146. (1) Ackermann2012a , (2) Gao2004 , (3) Greve2006 , (4) Kavanagh2020 , (5) Karachentsev2017 , (6) Kramer2020 , (7) Lamastra2019 , (8) Lopez2018 , (9) Peretti2019 , (10) Roy2010 , (11) Sanders2003 , (12) Sargent1987 , (13) Wang2018 , (14) Tang2017 , (15) Tully2009 , (16) Ahn2012 , (17) Skrutskie2006 , (18) Abazajian2009 ,(19) Meyer2004 , (20) Falco1999 , (21) van2016 ,(22) Izotova1999 , (23) McConnachie2012 ,(24) Paturel2002 Two-zone Model fit for NGC 1068 and NGC 4945 h As can be seen in Figure Fig2 , we found that the one-zone model did not explain the hardening spectral components from NGC 1068 and NGC 4945. Therefore, we considered a two-zone model, which assumes that they have two kinds of different protonic compositions from the two different zones. All protons inside the two zones satisfy the ECPL energy distribution. The two zones contributed to the soft and hard spectral compositions in the 0.1 to 500 GeV band. As shown in Figure Fig3 , by fitting their SEDs, we found that this two-zone model can better explain the hardening components of the SEDs of NGC 1068 and NGC 4945 than the one-zone model. Their best-fit results are given in Table Tab3 . h Spectral Feature All sources are classified into three categories, including SBG with the likely contribution from internal AGN (SBG-AGN), SBG without AGN (SBG), and SFR. For SBG-AGN, there are three objects: Circinus Guo2019 , NGC 3424 Peng2019 , and Arp 299 Xi2020 . Here, we first analyzed the spectral features of the three types of sources. For SBGs, we excluded NGC 4945 and NGC 1068 because they cannot be well explained by the one-zone model; we excluded Arp 220 because its fifth energy bin was associated with a higher flux than the fourth energy. By calculating the average values of the spectral index of NGC 253, M82, and NGC 2146, we found that the value was approximately 2.30. For SBG-AGNs and SFRs, their average values of the spectral index were 2.19 and 2.43, respectively. These results suggest that the spectral features of SBGs and SBG-AGNs are hard, and there is not much difference between them; thus, we suggest that the internal hidden AGNs from SBG-AGNs may not have a significant contribution to the GeV high-energy ray emission. The spectrum of M31 is currently hard, though there are only two data points with TS value > 4. We suggest that its particles may be in a primary acceleration stage because the process of particle acceleration from internal SNRs generally requires a long timescale from hundreds to thousands of years after the SNR explosion Yuan2018 . In the current Fermi LAT observation period of approximately 11 years, its particles may not be accelerated to a high-energy band, which makes the E_ cutoff value of the SED of M31 appear at about 0.03 TeV.

sources.summary: The paper highlights M31 as a key EXTraS field, where many XMM observations enabled searches for X-ray pulsations and new extragalactic pulsar detections.

quotes.quote: We do not describe these findings in this section, but show and briefly discuss the case of the nearby Galaxy M31. With tens of XMM archival observations, its relatively large number of X-ray sources, and properties similar to those of the Milky Way, M31 is one of the best regions of the sky in which to search for X-ray pulsations. In particular, despite the similarities with the Milky Way and the extensive monitoring of M31 since the Einstein mission, no accreting X-ray pulsar was found before the beginning of the EXTraS project. Within the project, 85 pointings of M31 have been analysed, and signals were searched for among 14438 detections (with more than 50 counts) and 36584 FFTs.

sources.summary: The paper links M31 to excess FRB dispersion measures, arguing that Local Group halo gas and a hot-gas bridge toward M31 and M33 can contribute substantially.

quotes.quote: If we consider only the CHIME FRBs that intersect the halos of Local Group galaxies M31 and M33, we still see a considerable excess DM.

If the Local Group IGrM is modeled with a dark matter halo mass of 10^ 12.5 M_ , it likely contributes a DM of >100 pc cm ^ 3 over tens of degrees on the sky in the direction of M31 . Our data are also consistent with recent analyses of X-ray O VII and O VIII emission lines and SZ maps that indicate significant hot-gas content extended to an angular radius of approximately 30 degrees from M31 . This is suggested to represent a hot-gas bridge connecting the Milky Way with M31 and M33 that corresponds to a DM of 80--400 pc cm ^ 3 .

sources.summary: The paper describes M31 as the parent of M110 and M32, with halo material stripped from those satellites through tidal interactions and encounters.

quotes.quote: M110, as well as M32, are satellites of M31. Evidence suggests that substantial stellar material (found in the halo of M31) has been stripped from these satellite galaxies after tidal interactions with M31 Ibata_2001 . As a matter of fact, Jarrett_et_al_2003 also pointed out the particular case of M110, arguing that its radial surface brightness is dominated by an exponential disc, which reinforces the issue that M110 was fitted with two components (see E_gals_double ). Regarding the compact elliptical galaxy M32 (cE2), it is classified as classical bulge because of the KR and n criteria, and, similarly to M110, it was modelled with two components [ an exponential disc as an additional component; actually, Graham_2002 reported that its surface brightness distribution is well described by a bulge plus an exponential disc profile ] . Besides, it has been proposed that it was disc galaxy with initial luminosity close to that of our Galaxy, which was disrupted due to an encounter with Andromeda galaxy DSouza_2018 . So, this may be the reason why M32 is falling almost in the 3 boundary of the KR (see Figure KR_final ). Hence, the low values in surface brightness, S ersic index and velocity dispersion of M110, as well as the case of M32, can be a consequence of tidal interactions with M31.

sources.summary: The paper discusses M31 as the key CMB Doppler-shift case where halo virial clouds would imprint blue/red shifts, later mapped with WMAP and Planck as galaxy rotation evidence.

quotes.quote: There were two proposals : (a) look for ray scintillation due to cosmic rays striking these clouds; (b) look for Doppler shifts in the CMB as seen through M31. Though the rays were seen, they had too many possible sources . The clouds in the halo of M31 rotating towards us would be blue-shifted and the part moving away would be red-shifted. WMAP data of 2011 showed the effect, at a low but significant level . Later, Planck data of 2014 confirmed the effect in the M31 halo at a sufficient accuracy, to provide convincing evidence which was further used to map the rotation of the M31 galaxy .

sources.summary: The paper describes M31 as a Milky-Way-like, highly inclined nearby spiral whose distance and infrared maps make it useful for resolving and studying supernova-remnant dust.

quotes.quote: M31 is very similar to the Milky Way in both metallicity and type, which is the largest external spiral galaxy in the local group and classified as an SA(s)b spiral 1991rc3..book.....D . The high inclination of i 78 ^ of M31 raises the surface brightness, and increases the contrast with foreground and background emission. At a distance of d = 744 kpc 2010A&A...509A..70V , M31 provides an opportunity to study the SNRs with spatial resolution that is surpassed only by the Magellanic Clouds. Previous studies of far-infrared (FIR) emission from the dust in M31 include maps made with Infrared Astronomical Satellite IRAS; 1984ApJ...278L..59H,1994AJ....108.1667D , the Infrared Space Observatory ISO; 1998A&A...338L..33H , Spitzer 2006ApJ...638L..87G , and Herschel 2012A&A...546A..34F .

sources.summary: The paper models gamma-ray production in M31, arguing that star-formation-driven cosmic rays transported into the CGM can explain extended emission from the galaxy.

quotes.quote: We discuss the production of rays from cosmic rays (CR) in the circumgalactic medium (CGM) of Andromeda (M31) in light of the recent detection of rays from an annular region of 5.5-120 kpc away from the M31 disc. We consider the CRs accelerated as a result of the star-formation in the M31 disk, which are lifted to the CGM by advection due to outflow and CR diffusion. T he advection time scale due to bulk flow of gas triggered by star formation activity in the M31 disc is comparable ( Gyr) to the diffusion time scale with diffusion coefficient 10^ 29 cm ^2 s ^ 1 for the propagation of CR protons with energy 412 GeV that are responsible for the highest energy photons observed.

We find that a combination of these mechanisms, that are related to the star formation processes in M31 in the last Gyr, along with diffusion and hadronic interaction, can explain the observed flux from the CGM of M31.

sources.summary: The paper cites M31 as the first non-Milky-Way test for an asymmetric CMB Doppler signal from halo clouds, saying WMAP/Planck detections were precise enough to study its rotation.

quotes.quote: An alternative suggestion was to look for an asymmetric Doppler shift of these clouds in M31 . The predicted effect was seen at a sufficient but low level in the WMAP data in 2011 and confirmed in the Planck data in 2014 . Objections that the blue shift may be due to ``hot spots" were countered by the argument that the red shift could not be ``cold spots" and were soon dispelled. The precision was sufficient to be able to use for studying the rotation of the M31.

sources.summary: The paper treats M31 as the primary system relative to which Tucana may be a distant backsplash satellite and M33 an orbiting perturber.

quotes.quote: Tucana's radial velocity is consistent with being at rest relative to M31. Further, Tucana is located close to M33's orbital plane around M31, and simple orbit integrations indicate that Tucana may have been ejected during an early pericentric passage of M33 11 Gyr ago, a timing which approximately coincides with Tucana's last episode of star formation. We suggest that Tucana may have been an early-infalling satellite of M31 or M33, providing a compelling explanation for its puzzling lack of gas and ongoing star formation despite its isolated nature. In this scenario, M33 should have completed some orbits around M31, a result that may help to explain the relative dearth of M33 satellite-candidates identified so far.

sources.summary: The review recounts Opik’s use of M 31 to demonstrate that the Andromeda Nebula is a massive stellar galaxy comparable in scale to the Milky Way.

quotes.quote: Opik started with the assumption that the Andromeda Nebula M 31 ``consists of stellar matter similar to the matter of our Galaxy,'' with the same ratio of mass to luminosity as the estimate for the Milky Way. That combined with the angular size of M 31, its apparent magnitude, and the measured rotation velocity, from the Doppler shift, yields a useful estimate of the distance and mass of this galaxy. (Showing how this follows is a good exercise for the student.) Opik's distance is half the correct value, an impressive advance at the time, and clear evidence that M 31 is a massive galaxy of stars, comparable to the size of the Milky Way.

sources.summary: The paper uses Andromeda as an external comparison: Dey et al. detect halo phase-space chevrons, connect the Giant Stellar Stream to recent tidal debris, and use M31 halo folds to constrain the potential.

quotes.quote: A demonstration of this idea is presented in Dey et al (2022) who detect multiple phase-space chevrons in the halo of the Andromeda galaxy. Enjoying the wide and dense spectroscopic coverage provided by the recently launched DESI survey they show that the Andromeda's Giant Stellar Stream is a part of a complex system of tidal debris from a massive and recent accretion event. Given the merger's relatively early stage, in contrast to that of the GS/E, the chevrons identified by Dey et al (2022) are fewer, more pronounced and are set further apart from each other in the phase space compared to the sub-structures discovered here. Dey et al (2022) take advantage of the panoramic view of the entire M31 halo and use the phase-space folds to constrain the M31's potential out to > 100 kpc from its centre.

sources.summary: The paper estimates M31 virial masses from four satellite galaxies, improving precision and arguing a high M31 mass affects Local Group dynamics, merger history, and satellite interpretation.

quotes.quote: We present new mass estimates for Andromeda (M31) using the orbital angular momenta of four satellite galaxies (M33, NGC 185, NGC 147, IC 10) derived from existing proper motions, distances, and line-of-sight velocities. We infer two masses for M31: M_ vir = 2.85^ +1.47 _ 0.77 10^ 12 M_ using satellite galaxy phase space information derived with HST based M31 proper motions and M_ vir =3.02^ +1.30 _ 0.69 10^ 12 M_ using phase space information derived with the weighted average of HST+Gaia based M31 proper motions. The precision of our new M31 mass estimates (23-50 ) improves by a factor of two compared to previous mass estimates using a similar methodology with just one satellite galaxy and places our results amongst the highest precision M31 estimates in recent literature.
An M31 mass > 2.5 10^ 12 M_ could have major implications for our understanding of LG dynamics, M31's merger and accretion history, and our understanding of LG galaxies in a cosmological context.

sources.summary: The review notes abundance-ratio evidence for M31, possible differences in its thin/thick disk dichotomy, and a likely accretion of a massive gas-rich satellite.

quotes.quote: Recently, similar relations are also shown for M31 using planetary nebulae. Since Fe is not available, Ar is used as a significant fraction of Ar is produced by SNe Ia. The thin and thick disk dichotomy seems not exactly the same, probably due to M31 experiencing an accretion of a relatively massive, gas-rich satellite galaxy arnaboldi22 .
The PFS will also be able to explore the /Fe bimodality in M31; it is not yet known if M31 has a similar /Fe dichotomy or not.

sources.summary: The paper uses reported 3.55 keV X-ray line detections from M31 to constrain sterile-neutrino dark matter, citing M31 mass uncertainties as expanding the inferred mixing-angle range.

quotes.quote: A candidate X-ray decay signal from a putative 7.1 keV-mass particle has been reported in galaxy clusters, the M31 galaxy and in the halo of the Milky Way (MW) e.g. Boyarsky14a,Bulbul14,Cappelluti17,Hofmann19 .

For the m_ s =7.1 keV sterile neutrino the distribution is maximally cold at 2 10^ 10 , which is also the upper limit on expected from the reported M31 3.55 keV line detection when the maximum uncertainty on the M31 mass is applied Boyarsky14a .

The first reported detections of an X-ray line at 3.55 keV consistent with dark matter decay were made by Bulbul14 in clusters of galaxies with the XMM-Newton (` XMM ') observatory and Boyarsky14a in the M31 galaxy and the Perseus galaxy cluster, also with XMM . The values of were estimated as 6.8^ +1.4 _ 1.4 10^ 11 ( 1 errors) for the cluster stack and 4.9^ +1.6 _ 1.3 10^ 11 for M31 on flux statistical uncertainties alone; including M31 mass uncertainties gives [ 2-20 ] 10^ 11 .

sources.summary: The review discusses M31 as having extended gamma-ray halo emission reaching about 200 kpc, with possible origins in bubbles or cosmic rays in its circumgalactic medium.

quotes.quote: Recent studies have reported detections of extended halo emission around M31 in rays. It extends up to 200 kpc from the center . It is possible that some of this extended emission originates from bubbles analogous to the Galactic Fermi bubbles , but most can be attributed to M31's CGM. The exact origin of this emission remains unsettled, however leptonic models with in situ CR acceleration in the acceleration shock due to in-falling matter , hadronic models involving CR advection and hadronic models including both CR advection and diffusion have been considered .

sources.summary: The paper motivates M31 as a complementary external disk galaxy whose proximity, edge-on view, rich halo substructure, and recent accretion history make it ideal for halo studies.

quotes.quote: In this regard, studying our neighbor, the Andromeda Galaxy (M31) is complementary. Its proximity ( 780 kpc) and edge-on orientation ( i 77^ ) offer a panoramic view and make it an ideal candidate for studying stellar haloes of external disk galaxies. The stellar halo of M31 shows a wealth of substructures, as well as numerous surviving satellites e.g Martin+2009, Richardson+2011, McConnachie+2018_PandasLSSII , suggesting a busy recent accretion history. There is evidence of a major recent accretion event that happened <4 Gyr ago with an external galaxy of mass 10^ 10 M_ e.g., Hammer+2018, Bhattacharya+2019_PNeII .

sources.summary: The paper selects the Andromeda galaxy as an IceCube dark-matter-decay target because of its proximity and large dark matter content, using an NFW halo model, an 8 degree ROI, and derived lifetime limits.

quotes.quote: The Andromeda galaxy (M31) is also chosen as a target, due to its proximity and large dark matter content. We take the NFW dark matter halo model for this galaxy presented in Ref. .
For the Andromeda galaxy, we limit the _ ROI to 8^ in order to separate the ROI from the Galactic Plane.
The best limits from this work are obtained by stacking the three galaxy clusters, and the next best limits by using the Andromeda galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper describes Andromeda as one of several nearby galaxies with satellite galaxies in rotating planar structures, arguing that such systems create strong tension for CDM models.

quotes.quote: (T4a) The Impossible Local Group: Disks of Satellites / Planes of Satellites : The Galaxy LyndenBell1976, Kroupa+2005, Metz+2008, PawlowskiKroupa2020 , Andromeda Koch+2006, Metz+2007, Ibata+2013 , Centaurus A Tully+2015, Mueller+2021 , M81 Chiboukas+2013 , NGC 253 Martinez+2021 and the galaxy pair NGC 4490/85 (Karachentsev Kroupa, submitted) and most other galaxies (Ibata et al. 2014 ) have a large fraction of their satellite galaxies arranged in vast rotating planar structures. Since the orbits of the satellites are confined to disks, these systems are reminiscent of planetary systems requiring a dissipational process for their formation rather than the spheroidal distribution of dark-matter bearing satellite galaxies expected from the merger tree in the SMoC (Pawlowski 2021a,b ).
The combined tension of only three (the Galaxy, Andromeda and Centaurus A) existing simultaneously is above the 5 sigma confidence (Asencio et al. 2022, ).

Ignoring that the Milky Way, Andromeda and Cen A actually have more satellite galaxies in their disk configurations than only 11 in each, observing three such independent plane-of-satellite systems simultaneously in our immediate cosmological vicinity would have a probability of 8 10^ 8 (consistent with the more accurate calculation available in Asencio et al. 2022 ).

sources.summary: The review says M31 had spectroscopic modeling problems and high mass-to-light ratios that supported the presence of a roughly 3-7 x 10^7 solar-mass SMBH.

quotes.quote: were able to report similar pieces of evidence for the galaxy M31 and the dwarf elliptical M32. Regardless of the geometry and the stellar dynamics included in their models, the authors were not able to reasonably reproduce the spectroscopic observations of these nearby galaxies. Indeed, similar issues to the ones of were reported. The mass-to-light ratios of these galaxies were too high and supported the idea that both M31 and M32 harboured SMBH of 3 7 10^7 and 8 10^6 , respectively .

sources.summary: The paper considers Andromeda galaxy as a UHECR source and Milky Way interaction partner, noting its more massive SMBH and arguing AGN outflows may have built a multi-Mpc hot halo.

quotes.quote: In this case, a contribution at the highest energies from more distant nearby sources like Cen A radio galaxy or Andromeda galaxy can play a role.

For example, one can expect such a deviation because of the interaction with the Andromeda galaxy and because our Galaxy is moving in the direction of Andromeda. SMBH in the Andromeda galaxy is 50 times more massive than SMBH in the Galactic center. So it is expected that outflows driven by AGN activity during the growth of Andromeda's SMBH produced a huge extended halo of the hot gas with a size of several Mpc.

sources.summary: The paper uses Andromeda galaxy (M31) as a dark-matter-halo lensing example, adopting its central black-hole mass and distance and finding small Burkert/Brownstein contributions to image positions.

quotes.quote: Besides the Sgr*A in the Milky Way, it is also important to see the gravitational lensing of Andromeda galaxy (M31) could provide a interesting example. According to the recent works in the Andromeda galaxy (M31) , the mass of the supermassive black hole is M=1.4 10^8M_ , the distance between the observer and the lens plane is D_ OL =785 kpc, which is exactly the distance between the earth and Andromeda galaxy (M31). The characteristic radius h and density _0 of dark matter halos corresponding to the Beta, Burkert, Brownstein, and Moore models are given in Table Table M31 .

It shows that the dark matter halos in Burkert and Brownstein models contribute little to the visual angular positions of lensed images for the supermassive black hole in the center of the Andromeda galaxy (M31).

sources.summary: The paper cites Andromeda as a key external-galaxy stream benchmark, where surveys measured stream velocities and distances and M31 giant-stream modelling inferred the halo mass.

quotes.quote: We have already observed and characterized numerous stellar streams around Andromeda due to its proximity 2009Natur.461...66M . With photometric and spectroscopic surveys such as PAndAS 2018ApJ...868...55M and SPLASH 2009ApJ...705.1275G , we have been able to obtain accurate radial velocity and distance measurements e.g. 10.1093/mnras/stw513, Preston_2019 for many of its streams. This has allowed a number of constraints on M31's potential, making it a key reference point for stream modelling in external galaxies. fit the giant stellar stream (GSS) in M31 with N-body simulations and existing GSS radial velocity and distance measurements with which they inferred the mass of M31's halo, M_ 200 10^ 12.27 0.1 M_ .

sources.summary: The paper cites prior modeling in which M31, assumed to have hosted a past AGN, accumulated positrons in its gaseous halo, producing a 511 keV flux near INTEGRAL sensitivity.

quotes.quote: This is a continuation of our previous work (Paper I) where we applied the above idea to an individual galaxy M31. Under assumption that it hosted an AGN in the past, we calculated the flux of 511 keV photons from it, which originate from annihilation of positrons accumulated in the gaseous halo over the lifetime of the galaxy. The resulting flux appeared to be of the order of INTEGRAL point source sensitivity. However, the source is expected to span approximately 1 in the sky which could hinder the detection.

sources.summary: The paper uses M31 SSS detections to infer a much larger absorbed population, estimating 800-5000 sources and a minimum total SSS luminosity near 8e39 erg/s.

quotes.quote: During the early 1990s, the ROSAT observatory established the class of SSSs by discovering additional Magellanic Cloud SSSs, as well as M31 SSSs and a small number in the Galaxy GreinerSSS .

DiStefano94 used the ROSAT-observed SSSs to estimate the size of the underlying populations in the Magellanic Clouds, M31, and in the Galaxy. They constructed several models for the distributions of gas and dust in each of these systems, and then seeded each galaxy with sources having the properties of the observed SSSs. The range of galaxy models allowed an estimate of the range of population sizes. It was found that in the Magellanic Clouds, the 4 ROSAT-observed SSSs could represent an total population of similar SSSs as small as 22 and as large as 100 . (These are sources that would be ``on'', but not detectable because of absorption.) The total SSS population numbers in the Milky Way range from 400 to 2900, with a most likely value of 1000 . Because the numbers of SSSs reported in M31 was large ( 25 ), and the distance is greater, thereby reducing the count rate, the estimated population of SSSs was larger: 800-5000 with a likely value of 2500 .

If we take 10^ 37 erg s ^ 1 to be a typical SSS luminosity, the Milky Way and M31 would have minimum SSS total luminosities (i.e. using the lowest possible estimated fractions) of 4 10^ 39 erg s ^ 1 and 8 10^ 39 erg s ^ 1 , respectively.

sources.summary: M 31 is used as a Local Group SBF benchmark: its images show measurable clumpiness, historical count-brightness population measurements, and SBF colors comparable to Virgo galaxies.

quotes.quote: This effect can be easily recognized by visual inspection of images of nearby galaxies like M 31 and M 32, in which the small-scale clumpiness can be a few percent of the mean surface brightness.

In fact, the use of SBFs for stellar population studies arguably preceded its use as a distance indicator: 1955AJ.....60..247B used the "count-brightness ratio," the ratio of the number of resolved stars to the integrated light, to study the populations of M 31 and M 32.

Also, the Local Group galaxies (M 31 and M 32) have SBF colors comparable to those of the Virgo galaxies, despite the very different physical apertures used in the measurements, since Virgo is about 20 times farther away than M 31 and M 32. This implies that the effects of any SBF color gradients in these galaxies are small.

sources.summary: The paper discusses M31's asymmetric nucleus, noting that TIGER and OASIS observations locate the black hole, reveal missed kinematic axes, and motivate non-axisymmetric modeling.

quotes.quote: The nucleus of M31 has been known to be asymmetric since 1974 . Observations with the integral-field spectrograph TIGER revealed that the black hole resides in the secondary peak in the brightness distribution , and led to models with an asymmetric distribution of stars . Measurements with OASIS on the CFHT have shown that HST spectroscopy with FOC , FOS and STIS along the apparent symmetry axis of the eccentric structure has in fact missed the kinematic major axis. A comprehensive non-axisymmetric model that fits this data will teach us much about the structure and formation of this nearby nucleus.

sources.summary: The paper uses M31 to define the Local Group barycentre, notes its small Hubble-law deviation, and discusses M31/Galaxy mass ratio and halo-mass constraints.

quotes.quote: By assuming the mass ratio of M31 and Galaxy is M_ M31 /M_ G 2 Sandage set the barycentre to be on the line between M31 and Galaxy at 2/3 of the distance to M31 .
On the other hand M31 deviates only little from the Hubble law (in fact it is within the 1 limit).

We have checked the behaviour of the velocity dispersion _v as a function of the position of the barycentre between Galaxy and M31 . Recently Evans et al. ( ) claimed that the halo of M31 is about as massive as the halo of Galaxy .

sources.summary: The paper models M31's inner gas flow with a rotating triaxial bulge, matching CO data and inferring a fast bulge, maximum disk, and low-density dark halo.

quotes.quote: The interstellar gas flow in the inner disk of M31 is modelled using a new, two dimensional, grid based, hydrodynamics code. The potential of the stellar bulge is derived from its surface brightness profile. The bulge is assumed to be triaxial and rotating in the same plane as the disk in order to explain the twisted nature of M31 's central isophotes and the non circular gas velocities in the inner disk. Results are compared with CO observations and the bulge is found to be a fast rotator with a B band mass-to-light ratio, _B = 6.5 0.8, and a ratio of co-rotation radius to bulge semi-major axis, R = 1.2 0.1, implying that any dark halo must have a low density core in contradiction to the predictions of CDM.

sources.summary: The paper uses deep HST photometry to study M31 disk and halo stellar populations, finding complex populations, metal-rich RGB stars, and little old-halo metallicity variation.

quotes.quote: We have obtained HST-WFPC2 F555W and F814W photometry for 16 fields in the vicinity of the luminous nearby spiral galaxy M31, sampling the stellar content of the disk and the halo at different distances from the center, from 20 to 150 arcmin (i.e. 4.5 to 35 kpc), down to limiting V and I magnitudes of 27. The Color-Magnitude diagrams (CMD) obtained for each field show the presence of complex stellar populations, including an intermediate age/young population and older populations with a wide range of metallicity. Those fields superposed on the disk of M31 generally show a blue plume of stars which we identify with main sequence members.

Our first round of large-scale HST imaging gives evidence that the old ``halo'' stellar population is more metal rich than that of the Milky Way, with surprisingly little variation in the properties of the old stellar population, even for fields ranging up to galactocentric distances exceeding 30 kpc.

sources.summary: The paper compares M31 with M32, finding stronger Ca4227 and related indices but similar derived calcium-to-alpha ratios, while interpreting M32 CaT differences as age-driven.

quotes.quote: The galaxies M31 and M32 are labelled in Fig. fig:indices . M31 has stronger Ca4227 and \ indices than M32, so that both objects have very similar \ ratios ( [ ] 0 ). Curiously, M32 shows stronger CaT absorption than M31 KB99 , which seems to indicate higher calcium abundance in M32. As M32 is younger than M31 Traetal00b , the strong CaT absorption index of M32 is most likely caused by an increase of Paschen line absorption.

sources.summary: The paper describes M31 as the nearest spiral galaxy, with XMM-Newton observations of its central region and an X-ray source population dominated by point sources.

quotes.quote: Four XMM-Newton X-ray observations of the central region of the Andromeda Galaxy (M31) have revealed an X-ray source that varies in luminosity over 1--3 10 ^ 38 erg s ^ 1 between observations and also displays significant variability over time-scales of a few hundred seconds.

At 760 kpc vdb00 the Andromeda Galaxy (M31) is the nearest spiral galaxy to our own. The X-ray emission from the direction of M31 is dominated by point sources, mostly X-ray binaries (XB), with a mixture of supernova remnants, foreground stars and background AGN. The intensity variations on short and long time-scales in the X-ray sources in M31 provide vital clues to their nature, and in some cases, in combination with X-ray spectral information, they can be classified from the X-ray observations alone.

sources.summary: The paper compares a dark-matter-driven black-hole growth estimate with M 31, using its roughly constant velocity dispersion and finding the predicted mass comparable to observed black-hole masses.

quotes.quote: However, in galaxies M 31 and NGC 4258 it seems to be almost constant and equals approximately 200 km/s . Taking the value of f_0 the same as in our Galaxy ( f_0 ) and the time of growth t=3 10^ 17 s = 10^ 10 yr. we obtain the value M_ bh = 1.8 10^7 M_ . The observed black hole masses are (2.0 8.5) 10^7 M_ for M 31 and 3.8 10^7 M_ for NGC 4258 . The comparison shows that dark matter may comprise significant fraction of black hole masses in these galaxies.

sources.summary: The paper uses M31 as a nearby comparison for disk-galaxy halos, noting metal-enriched halo substructure, difficult bulge-halo decomposition, and spheroid profiles steeper than its stacked-galaxy result.

quotes.quote: Recent work by has demonstrated the presence of substructure in the moderately metal-enriched stellar halo of M31, that is likely to be the relic of the disruption of one or more small companion galaxy.

Although its small distance makes M31 the easiest target for observing of the halo population of a disk galaxy, the prominence of its bulge makes it hard to disentangle the density distribution of the halo so that it is perhaps more appropriate to talk about a generic spheroid. measured the SB of M31 spheroid to _V 30 mag arcsec ^ 2 and concluded that it can be modelled either by a de Vaucouleurs law or, in its outer parts, by a power-law ^ 5 , which is much steeper than what we find. However, the globular cluster distribution follows ^ 3 racine91 and there is evidence for a shallower power-law index in the outer parts of the halo from more recent observations (Irwin, private communication).

sources.summary: The review describes M31 as a nearby inclined spiral whose molecular gas surveys now resolve spiral structure, molecular cloud populations, and kinematic constraints on bulge properties.

quotes.quote: M31 M31 is the nearest spiral to our own, offering an alternative view of a large, bright spiral galaxy. The large inclination of M31 motivates the study of molecular gas at high angular resolution, in order to separate arm and interarm regions and assess the properties and development of spiral structure. Recent efforts to attain a complete assessment of the molecular gas in M31 have seen a dramatic improvement in spatial resolution, from the first complete CO survey at 1.7 kpc resolution in 1993 (with the CfA 1.2m, ) to a higher resolution (200 pc) survey of the southwestern half of the galaxy (with the FCRAO 14m, , ) and a full survey at better than 90pc resolution (with the IRAM 30m, ) in the last few years. We can now assess the properties of the spiral structure and molecular cloud population in M31, and compare it with those of the Milky Way (see presentation by M.

sources.summary: The paper uses M 31 as a benchmark galaxy with a nuclear black hole, estimating its black-hole mass and the nuclear-cluster density threshold needed for AGN-like accretion.

quotes.quote: Consider three well studied galaxies having a firm evidence of presence of a nuclear b.h.: our Milky Way, M 31 and the giant elliptical M 87 in Virgo. The modern estimates of the central black hole masses available in the literature are, respectively, 2.6 10^6 M _ , 6.2 10^7 M _ and 3.6 10^9 M _ .

Assuming <v_*> =10 kms ^ 1 for we get _c= 3.4 10^7 M _ pc ^ 3 for the MW, _c=5 10^5 M _ pc ^ 3 for M 31 and _c=300 M _ for M 87. The value of _c for M87 is clearly a normal-low value for the central density of galactic GCs, while the _c of M 31, even if large, is compatible with the estimated central values of some non core-collapsed GCs (see the Harris web Catalog of parameters for Milky Way globular clusters, feb. 2003, http://physun.physics.mcmaster.ca/ harris/mwgc.dat).

sources.summary: The paper measures a TRGB distance to M31 using halo RGB stars and avoids crowding, disk/bulge contamination, and substructure.

quotes.quote: M31, the Andromeda Galaxy M31 ( 0 h  42 m  44.3 s , +41^  16'  9'' ) is the closest giant spiral galaxy to the Milky Way. It provides a unique opportunity to study in detail a galaxy that is thought to be similar to our own. All the stellar populations that one would expect to find in a large disk galaxy can be found in M31, and a detailed discussion of this vast and spectacular object is well beyond the scope of this paper. and Irwin et al., in preparation , present maps of the spatial distribution of red giant branch stars as seen in our INT WFC survey of this galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper describes M31 associations as large star complexes containing OB associations, with NGC 206 cited as a superassociation and Battinelli et al. finding hierarchical stellar groupings.

quotes.quote: Baade (1963) considered the most active examples of star complexes and called them ``superassociations,'' such as 30 Dor in the LMC and NGC 206 in M31.

Efremov noted that the OB associations in M31 defined by van den Bergh (1981) have an average size of 480 pc, which is much larger than local OB associations (80 pc). He concluded that the M31 associations are complexes, and that star complexes generally contain OB associations as sub-parts. Battinelli et al. (1996) quantitatively found stellar groupings in M31 and demonstrated this 2-component, or hierarchical, nature of associations and complexes.

sources.summary: The paper models M31's giant southern stream, using the galaxy potential to constrain stream orbits and infer the mass within 125 kpc of M31.

quotes.quote: We construct test-particle orbits and simple N-body models that match the properties of the giant stellar stream observed to the south of M31, using the model of M31's potential derived in the companion paper by Geehan (2005). We introduce a simple approximation to account for the difference in position between the stream and the orbit of the progenitor; this significantly affects the best-fitting orbits. The progenitor orbits we derive have orbital apocenter 60 and pericenter 3 , though these quantities vary somewhat with the current orbital phase of the progenitor which is as yet unknown. Our best combined fit to the stream and galaxy properties implies a mass within 125 kpc of M31 of (7.4 1.2) 10^ 11 .

sources.summary: The paper frames M31 as a nearby giant spiral whose globular-cluster system probes its formation, kinematics, outer halo, and mass distribution.

quotes.quote: The globular cluster system of M31 (the Andromeda galaxy) has been the subject of deep investigations since the dawn of extragalactic astronomy (Hubble ). M31 is the only giant spiral galaxy beyond the Milky Way that we can hope to study in some detail, and globular clusters (GCs) are generally recognized as excellent tracers of the physical properties of the parent stellar system (Hodge ). The comparative study of the GC systems of M31 and of the Milky Way may also provide very fruitful insights on the formation history of the parent galaxies as well as important constraint to theories of GC formation (see Harris , and references therein). Finally, the kinematics of GC provides a classical tool to estimate the mass distribution of the parent galaxy out to large distances (see, Evans et al. , and references therein, for the case of M31).

sources.summary: The paper uses CFHT Megacam observations to study M31's southern outer disk, identifying 361 new carbon-star candidates and tracing the disk edge and intermediate-age C-star population to large radii.
