quotes.quote: We employ the CFHT Megacam camera to survey one deg ^2 of the southern outer disc of M31, a region which includes the area where Battinelli et al. (2003) have identified nearly one thousand C stars. In the outer M31 region not previously surveyed, we identify 361 new C star candidates, having similar photometric properties to the known ones, and confirm the slight decrease in the luminosity of C stars with galactocentric distances.
Our uniform large field survey detects the edge of M31 disk at 35 kpc. The intermediate-age population, represented by C stars, extends further to 40 kpc.

sources.summary: The paper describes M31 as the Local Group's second major spiral, with stellar populations differing from the Milky Way but sharing a star-forming disk, bulge, halo, and globular clusters.

quotes.quote: We also have a second example of a spiral galaxy in the Local Group, namely M31. The stellar content of our Milky Way and M31 appear markedly different. For example, the stellar population in the halo of M31 is more metal rich in the mean than our halo. However, globally both these galaxies contain a large gaseous star-forming disc, a bulge and a halo and both galaxies have significant populations of globular clusters.

sources.summary: The paper flags M31 as unusual within its GALEX radial-profile sample, with a disturbed central FUV attenuation profile and inner star-formation-law points unlike the rest of the galaxies.

quotes.quote: The fact that we see in the individual A(FUV) profiles continuous trends with radius let us think that on average our extinction gradients are not much affected by such cases (in the figure of the appendix, only M31 and NGC0253 do present a strong disturbance in the central part of the A(FUV) profile).

In this figure, we also connected the points corresponding to M31. The inner 50'' of this galaxy are orthogonal to the usual Schmidt relation (and the one found in the rest of the sample). We leave for later studies to interpret this untypical behavior of one of our nearest neighbor.

sources.summary: The paper estimates M31's gamma-ray flux from its supernova rate and distance, compares it with detailed Local Group predictions, and says GLAST should significantly detect M31.

quotes.quote: For example, the supernova rate of Andromeda (M31), at a distance of 800 kpc, is 1 per century, compared to the rate of 2.5 every century in the Milky Way . Thus the expected ray photon flux from M31 should be at the level _ M31 (> 100 MeV ) 1 2.5 10^ 43 ph s ^ 1 4 (800 kpc )^2 0.9 10^ 8 L_ 39 cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 , for _ = -0.4 , which would be significantly detected with GLAST. Pavlidou and Fields (2001) perform a more detailed treatment of local group galaxies and predict that the > 100 MeV integral photon flux from the SMC, M31, and M33 are at the levels of 1.7 10^ 8 , 1.0 10^ 8 , and 0.11 10^ 8 ph ( > 100 MeV) cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 , respectively. GLAST should therefore detect at least the SMC and M31, though its 1 year sensitivity of 0.4 10^ 8 ph( >100 MeV) cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 means that few other local group galaxies are likely to be detected.

sources.summary: The paper treats M31 as a key spiral-galaxy distance-ladder target and reports an eclipsing-binary distance modulus consistent with earlier M31 determinations.

quotes.quote: M31 and M33, being the nearest spiral galaxies, are crucial stepping-stones in the extragalactic distance ladder. have determined the first distance to a spiral galaxy, specifically to a semi-detached system ( V=19.3 mag) in M31. Note that at such distances, the location of the binary within the galaxy has an insignificant effect on the distance ( <1 ). Light curves for the system in M31 were obtained from the survey of with the 2.5 meter Isaac Newton telescope and spectroscopy with the 8 meter Gemini telescope using GMOS. Such stars are at the limit of current spectroscopic capabilities. The resulting distance is 772 44 kpc and distance modulus is 24.44 0.12 mag, in agreement with previous distance determinations to M31.

sources.summary: The paper frames M 31 as the nearest giant spiral with metal-rich old and present-day populations, using A-type supergiants to trace disk abundance gradients and stellar evolution.

quotes.quote: A comprehensive global view of a giant spiral galaxy can be obtained only for the nearest neighbour of this class, the Andromeda galaxy (M 31). The old stellar population of this, the most luminous galaxy of the Local Group, turns out to be unexpectedly metal-rich (see e.g. van den Bergh 1999) and present-day abundances as traced by nebulae (H ii regions, supernova remnants) also indicate a metal-rich character and the presence of abundance gradients in the disk (Dennefeld Kunth 1981; Blair et al. 1982).

This makes BA-SGs highly valuable for constraining the galactochemical evolution of M 31 empirically by tracing the abundance gradients. However, there is more to gain: observational constraints on the evolution of massive stars in a metal-rich environment, using BSGs as probes for mixing with nuclear-processed matter, and the potential to employ them as distance indicators via application of the flux-weighted gravity-luminosity relationship (FGLR, Kudritzki et al. 2003).

sources.summary: The paper treats M31 as a large external galaxy whose outer globular clusters trace its halo, formation history, chemical evolution, kinematics, and mass distribution.

quotes.quote: Globular clusters observed in M31 provide the closest example of a globular cluster system belonging to a large external galaxy. Members of this system allow us to trace the star formation history, chemical evolution, kinematics, and mass distribution in different regions of M31, and are therefore vital to our developing picture of its formation and evolution. However, very few clusters have been discovered at large distances from M31, meaning that up until now we do not possess a good sample with which to probe its outer halo. Recently, as part of a major survey of the M31 halo with the Isaac Newton Telescope see e.g., ferguson:02 and MegaCam on the Canada-France-Hawaii Telescope see e.g., martin:06 , a search for previously unknown globular clusters, at large projected radii ( R_p ) from the center of M31, has been conducted.

sources.summary: The paper evaluates M31 as an external MOND-field source, giving its distance, baryonic mass, gravitational effect on the Milky Way, and limits on its contribution.

quotes.quote: There are two obvious possible contributions: the Andromeda galaxy M31, and the Large Scale Structure (LSS). Andromeda (e.g. Evans et al. 2000) is located at a distance d_A 800 kpc and has a baryonic mass M_A 10^ 11 M_ . This means that the gravitational force per unit mass exerted by M31 on the Milky Way is of the order of (GM_Aa_0)^ 1/2 d_A a_0/100, with some variations depending on the actual location considered in the Milky Way. However, since both galaxies have a roughly similar baryonic mass, the MOND gravitational field created by M31 is always smaller than the one created by the Milky Way in the direction opposite to M31, meaning that M31 cannot be the only source of the approximately constant external field we are looking for.

sources.summary: The paper treats Andromeda as a nearby Milky-Way-like spiral where dark-matter mini-spikes around IMBHs could appear as gamma-ray point sources detectable by GLAST or ACT scans.

quotes.quote: Here, we further explore the mini-spikes scenario, and focus on the population of IMBHs in the Andromeda Galaxy (also known as M31), a spiral galaxy very similar to the Milky Way (MW), whose center is located 784 kpc away from us. We compute gamma-ray fluxes from DM annihilations around IMBHs in M31, and show that the prospects for detection with GLAST are very promising: in an optimistic case (a neutralino with a mass m_ =150 GeV and annihilation cross section v = 3 10^ 26 cm ^ 3 s ^ 1 ), GLAST may detect up to 20 point-like sources (at 5 and with a 2 months exposure), within 3^ from the center of Andromeda.

In the case of mini-spikes in M31, ACTs can search for them by scanning a small region of 3^ around the center of M31, and an effective exposure of 100 hours in this region would be sufficient to probe the proposed scenario, at least for DM mass in the TeV range.
We then (Section sec:chapter three ) present the IMBHs catalogue and how it is adapted to the Andromeda Galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper presents realistic microlensing optical-depth maps for M31, modeling disk and bulge sources lensed by M31 and Galactic halos and studying halo-parameter effects.

quotes.quote: We provide a set of microlensing optical depth maps for M31. Optical depths towards Andromeda were calculated on the basis of a four component model of the lens and source populations: disk and bulge sources lensed by bulge, M31 halo and Galactic halo lenses. We confirm the high optical depth and the strong optical depth gradient along the M31 minor axis due to a dark halo of lenses and also discuss the magnitude of the self-lensing due to the bulge. We explore how the shape of the optical depth maps to M31 vary with the halo parameters core radius and flattening.

sources.summary: The paper presents SLOTT-AGAPE as a pixel-lensing microlensing survey targeting M31, using its dense stellar field to search for MACHOs and study dark-matter haloes around M31 and the Milky Way.

quotes.quote: \ SLOTT-AGAPE (Systematic Lensing Observation at Toppo Telescope - Andromeda Gravitational Amplification Pixel Lensing Experiment) is a new collaboration project among international partners from England, France, Germany, Italy and Switzerland that intends to perform microlensing observation by using M31 as target.
The AGAPE and Columbia-Vatican (VATT) collaborations look for microlensing in the direction of the M31 galaxy. This could yield very useful information on the haloes of both our own Galaxy and M31.
In a galaxy like M31, however, this is compensated by the very high density of stars.

sources.summary: The paper treats M31 as an uncertain contributor to the MOND external field, estimating its baryonic mass, distance, and external-field strength on the Milky Way.

quotes.quote: Indeed, the effect of M31, and of the Coma and Virgo clusters, makes the value of the external field extremely uncertain. For instance, the baryonic mass of M31 is estimated to lie between 7 10^ 10 M_ and 2 10^ 11 M_ (Seigar et al. 2006; Tempel et al. 2007; Geehan et al. 2006; Carignan et al. 2006), and sits at 800 kpc from the Milky Way center, thus exerting an external field in the range 0.01a_0-0.02a_0 , a value roughly similar to the one exerted by Large Scale Structure. Note however that the external field from M31 is a varying one, and that if one only considered the external field from M31, no star could ever escape from the MW-M31 system because the external field from M31 would never dominate over the internal one from the MW in the direction opposite to M31.

sources.summary: The paper describes M31 halo metallicity and its giant southern stream as Local Group benchmarks for diffuse intrahalo light, stellar halo abundance, and tidal-feature metallicity.

quotes.quote: M31's pressure-supported halo has an approximate iron abundance of [ Fe/H ] 1.4 see also Kalirai06 Chapman_etal06 , and it resembles the Milky Way halo both kinematically and structurally.
Recent measurements of stellar halo metallicity in the Local Group seem to cluster around the point Z 1.5 see also Kalirai06 for a review of the halo abundance of M31 Ferguson07 .

Tidal features such as M31's giant southern stream are rich in stellar mass the progenitor may have been as bright as a billion solar luminosities according to font_etal06m31 as well as metals Z 0.5 according to Guhathakurta_etal05 , so we can reconcile our predictions with current galactic measurements given the caveat that typical halo metallicities reflect the underlying stellar component, without the inclusion of bright outer-halo substructure resulting from massive accretion events, a distinction made more clear by a recent semi-analytic treatment of baryon dynamics in numerical models of L _* galaxy halos see also BullockJohnston05 for details of the simulation Font_etal07 .

sources.summary: The paper treats M31 as the foreground or neighboring galaxy whose distance, disk asymmetries, dust rings, reddening, and light contamination are central to testing M32's physical association.

quotes.quote: Since M 1950 , S 1954 and A 1964 , it has been known that M31's disc exhibits some rotation curve and optical asymmetries. Although R 1966 , RF 1970 and E 1974 showed these asymmetries to be much smaller than originally thought, the view that the two galaxies must be very close neighbours has been reinforced (a) by recent maps compiled by G+ 2006 that revealed asymmetries in M31's dust lanes (as distinct from its spiral arms) and (b) by computer simulations ( B 1976 through G+ 2006 ) that demonstrated that a gravitational encounter between M31 and M32 could produce these asymmetries .

Very recent numerical simulations by B+ 2006 suggest that two off-centre inner dust rings in M31 are likely to be density waves caused by a companion galaxy colliding head on with the centre of M31's disc 210 million years ago.

sources.summary: The paper treats M31 as a major Local Group constituent: the Milky Way mass is measured to half its distance, and binding the Local Group requires dark matter between the Galaxy and M31.

quotes.quote: The NFW model indicates that the Galaxy's mass within 385 kpc, half distance to M31, is 4 . High-velocity ends of the pseudo rotation curve for the entire Local Group up to 1.5 Mpc indicate isothermal nature with a terminal velocity of 200 . In order for the Local Group to be gravitationally bound, an order of magnitude larger mass than those of the Galaxy and M31 is required. This fact suggest that the Local Group contains dark matter of mass 5 , filling the space between the Galaxy and M31.

sources.summary: The paper compares M33 with M31, noting that M31 hosts globular clusters to much larger radii and that wide-field surveys have expanded but not completed its halo-cluster census.

quotes.quote: M31 hosts GCs at much larger radial distances, out to 200  kpc, however this population has only been uncovered in the last few years thanks to sensitive panoramic digital imaging surveys (e.g. Martinetal06, Huxoretal08 ). For example, using 84 square degrees of imaging data from the Isaac Newton Telescope Wide-Field Camera (INT/WFC) and Canada France Hawaii Telescope (CFHT) MegaCam surveys of M31, Huxoretal08 were able to increase the number of known GCs with projected radii 1 degree ( 14  kpc at M31's distance) by more than 75 ; this is especially remarkable given that most of this data came from a single outer quadrant of M31 and suggests that many more halo GCs remain to be discovered in that system.

Such objects are rather common in the outskirts of M31 (e.g. Huxoretal05, Huxoretal08 ) and would not have been automatically found by the method above as they do not appear as a single source in the object catalog.

sources.summary: The paper adapts Milky Way IMBH mini-spike catalogs to M31, predicting about 65 hosted IMBHs but noting that their detectability is limited by Andromeda's distance.

quotes.quote: The mock IMBH catalogs obtained for the Milky Way have been adapted, by a suitable rescaling, to the population of IMBHs hosted by the Andromeda galaxy , leading to the prediction that the M31 should host about 65 IMBHs (again this estimate strongly depends on the assumed cosmological parameters). Detectability toward Andromeda is reduced greatly due to the comparably large distance to the Andromeda galaxy. On the other hand, in the case of detecting a handful of such objects, they will all be located within 3^ from the center of Andromeda, which may be an interesting spatial signature (see Fig. fig:IMBHs_Andromeda ).

sources.summary: The paper infers M31 has a larger stellar mass and rotation velocity than the Milky Way, yielding a halo mass near 3 x 10^12 solar masses and consistency with the Local Group timing argument.

quotes.quote: M31 appears to have a larger stellar mass than the Milky Way, consistent with its larger maximum rotation velocity, and this translates into a larger inferred halo mass M_ halo = 3.0 10^ 12 M_ . It is interesting that the sum of the halo masses estimated for M31 and the Galaxy from our ``zero scatter'' abundance matching is close to the best estimate of the same quantity ( M_ halo (MW) + M_ halo (M31) = 5.3 10^ 12 M_ ) which obtained from a CDM-calibrated Timing Argument applied to the relative orbit of the two galaxies. Note, however, that if we allow maximal scatter in the M_* - M_ halo relation, then the Milky Way's halo mass could be as low as the values found in other recent MW analyses, and the sum M_ halo (MW) + M_ halo (M31) would still not violate the 90 confidence range quoted by .

sources.summary: The paper uses M31 to show that emission-measure criteria for diffuse ionized gas depend on local star-forming environment and position within a galaxy.

quotes.quote: To illustrate the above problem, an example is provided by the galaxy M31, for which Walterbos Braun (1994) adopted the EM criterion to distinguish DIGs from regions. They found that close to low star formation regions, the DIG always had an EM <50 pc cm^ 6 , while near higher star formation regions, the latter had an EM near 100 pc cm^ 6 . Therefore, even though the EM is proportional to the electron density and should thereby reflect how ``diffuse'' an emission region is, this criterion was shown to vary with position in this galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper describes M31 as an Sb spiral whose globular-cluster count predicts an SMBH mass consistent with the observed value, supporting the relation for spirals.

quotes.quote: The nearby Sb spiral galaxy M31 has 450 100 GCs (Table 1). According to equation ( eq:one ) this leads to a SMBH mass of (1.2 0.4) 10^8M_ , in excellent agreement with the observed value of 1.4^ +0.9 _ 0.3 10^8M_ . Thus the M_ - N_ GC correlation may work well for spiral galaxies as late as Hubble type Sb.

sources.summary: The paper describes M 31 as a radio-faint neighboring spiral with weak magnetic fields, a dominating axisymmetric disk field, and a future SKA rotation-measure target.

quotes.quote: Radio-faint galaxies like M 31 and M 33, our Milky Way's neighbors, have weaker total magnetic fields (5--7 G), while gas-rich spiral galaxies with high star-formation rates, like M 51, M 83 and NGC 6946, have total field strengths of 20--30 G in their spiral arms.
The Andromeda galaxy M 31 and several other galaxies host a dominating axisymmetric disk field, as predicted by dynamo models.
More than 10^4 RM values are expected in the area of M 31 and will allow the detailed reconstruction of the 3-D field structure in this and many other nearby galaxies.

sources.summary: The paper discusses M31 as M33's giant neighbor, the target of PAndAS halo mapping, and the likely tidal interaction partner shaping M33's substructure.

quotes.quote: However, given the distance of M31 ( D_ M31 = 785 25 kpc; mcconnachie2005a ) and the separation of M31 and M33 on the plane of the sky ( 15 degrees), it is reasonable to consider M33 as the most luminous satellite of M31.

Building upon these previous surveys, the Pan-Andromeda Archaeological Survey (PAndAS; mcconnachie2009b ), a Large Program on the CFHT, is contiguously mapping the entire halo of M31 out to a maximum projected radius of 150 kpc, and the surroundings of M33 out to a maximum projected radius of 50 kpc.
Our favored interpretation of the large substructure in M33 is that it is due to a a tidal interaction with M31.

sources.summary: The paper uses M31 as a key observational comparison for simulated stellar halos, emphasizing its disturbed, metal-rich halo, active merger history, streams, satellites, and metallicity gradient.

quotes.quote: In spite of this, questions have been raised about the compatibility with the standard paradigm of the seemingly large differences in the properties of the stellar haloes and satellite systems of M31 and the Milky Way. These two systems have similar masses but M31 appears to have experienced a much more active merger history in the recent past, as suggested by its more disturbed stellar disc, larger bulge, younger and more metal-rich stellar populations, larger and more numerous tidal streams and surviving satellite galaxies guhathakurta05,irwin05,brown06a,brown06b,gilbert06,kalirai06,mcconnachie06,gilbert07,ibata07,koch08,kalirai09 .
The halo of M31 also shows evidence for a gradient, where the inferred metallicity may drop by as much as 1  dex from the inner regions of the galaxy out to 100  kpc (see Fig.  fig:iron_profs below).

sources.summary: The review presents M 31 as a radio-faint neighboring spiral with weak total fields, a prototype axisymmetric dynamo field, and a target for dense future RM mapping.

quotes.quote: Radio-faint galaxies like M 31 (Fig. fig:m31 ) and M 33 taba08 , our Milky Way's neighbors, have weaker total magnetic fields (about 6 G), while gas-rich spiral galaxies with high star-formation rates, like M 51 and NGC 6946 (Fig. fig:m51 ), have total field strengths of 20-30 G in their spiral arms. The Andromeda galaxy M 31 (Fig. fig:m31rm ) is the prototype of a dynamo-generated axisymmetric spiral disk field fletcher04 which extends to 25 kpc distance from the center han98 . More than 10,000 RM values are expected in the area of M 31 and will allow the detailed reconstruction of the 3-D field structure.

sources.summary: The paper compares M31 with Milky Way analogues, finding its star formation rate, stellar mass, and color broadly consistent with a green-valley spiral.

quotes.quote: The star formation rate of M31 is much better established than that of the Milky Way.
The stellar mass and resulting u color of M31 is consistent with the green valley region of the full Zoo sample.
Our key observational results can be summarized as follows: M31 is broadly consistent with being a member of the green valley population of the color vs. stellar mass diagram.

sources.summary: The paper uses planetary-nebula spectra to measure M31 disk abundances and its radial oxygen gradient, finding the gradient shallower than the Milky Way's even after accounting for M31's larger disk scale length.

quotes.quote: We have obtained spectra of 16 PNe in the disk of M31 and determined the abundances of He, N, O, Ne, S and Ar. Here we present the median abundances and compare them with previous M31 PN disk measurements and with PNe in the Milky Way. We also derive the radial oxygen gradient in M31, which is shallower than that in the Milky Way, even accounting for M31's larger disk scale length.

sources.summary: The paper uses M31 to show that the planetary nebula luminosity function bright-end cutoff is invariant across bulge, inner disk, and outer disk/halo populations.

quotes.quote: To demonstrate this, one need only look at our Local Group neighbor, M31. As illustrated in Figure m31 , the PNLF of M31's bulge ( R < 1 kpc) has the same bright-end cutoff (defined via the absolute magnitude M^* ) as that of the system's inner disk ( 6 < R < 10 kpc) and outer disk/halo ( R > 15 kpc). Given the range of stellar population being observed, this invariance is remarkable.

sources.summary: The review uses the M31-M33 stellar bridge as nearby evidence that low-surface-brightness tidal debris can reveal underestimated galaxy interactions.

quotes.quote: See however on Figure fig:M31 the stellar bridge between Andromeda and M33. The debris, of extremely low surface brightness, are remnants of a tidal interaction between two of the most massive galaxies in our Local Group. Therefore the evaluation of the merger rate based on statistics on galaxy pairs should consider systems like M31/M33 as pairs even-though the two galaxies have a spatial separation has high as 220 kpc.

sources.summary: The paper models M31 as a gas-rich major-merger remnant whose tidal tail could have reached the Milky Way and formed nearby dwarf systems.

quotes.quote: Investigating from our modelling of M31 as a result of a former gas-rich major merger, we find that one of the expected tidal tail produced during the event may have reached the MW. Such a coincidence may appear quite exceptional, but the MW indeed lies within the small volume delineated by the tidal tail at the present epoch. In our scenario, most of the MW dwarves, including the MCs, may have been formed within a tidal tail formed during the former merger in the Local Group.

sources.summary: The paper finds no evidence for a dark-matter radio haze in M31 and notes that Andromeda should be more radio luminous under most dark-matter interpretations of the Milky Way haze.

quotes.quote: In Figures fig:m31overlay and fig:overlay we show the observed 1.485 GHz radio overlays for M31 and for the remainder of our galaxy sample respectively, noting that the flux contours for each follow the form 2 ^ n/2 mJy beam ^ 1 with an outermost contour of n = -2 for M31, and n = -3 for the remainder of our candidate galaxies.

Remarkably, one of the galaxies without evidence of a dark matter haze is our local-group neighbor, M31 (the Andromeda Galaxy). While it is difficult to turn this into a firm exclusion limit on the dark matter origin of the Milky Way haze, one would have expected Andromeda to be significantly more radio luminous in most dark matter interpretations of the WMAP haze.

sources.summary: The review describes M31's negative redshift toward the Milky Way and cites the timing argument that treats both galaxies as a bound system requiring extra mass.

quotes.quote: Kahn:1959 paid attention to the fact that most galaxies have positive redshifts as a result of the expansion of the Universe; only the Andromeda galaxy M31 has a negative redshift of about 120 km/s, directed toward our Galaxy. This fact can be explained if both galaxies form a physical system. From the approaching velocity, the mutual distance, and the time since passing the perigalacticon (taken equal to the present age of the Universe), the authors calculated the total mass of the double system, about 5 times the sum of the conventional masses of the Galaxy and M31.

sources.summary: The paper uses PNe to trace the Andromeda galaxy surroundings, surface-brightness profile, inner disk contribution, extended disk dominance, and intermediate-age stellar populations.

quotes.quote: In recent years the surroundings of \ have been mapped using deep ground-based photometric surveys of the Andromeda galaxy Fer02,Irwin05,Ibata07,McConnachie09 with the wide-field cameras of the Isaac Newton Telescope (INT) and the Canada France Hawaii Telescope (CFHT). Ibata05,Ibata07 presented a surface brightness profile for \ and concluded that along the minor axis, in the region 0.2^ <R<0.4^ , the classical inner (thin) disk of \ contributes to the profile, but at 0.5^ <R<1.3^ the extended disk component becomes dominant. Since PNe trace the distribution of the underlying intermediate-age stellar populations, we construct a PN density profile for \ using the catalogue of PNe we compiled from our sample and from the samples of HK04, H06, and M06 (see Section txt:compar ).

sources.summary: The paper treats Andromeda galaxy as one of the XMM-Newton targets showing an anomalous 3.5 keV X-ray line and argues decaying 7 keV axino dark matter could explain the signal.

quotes.quote: In this scenario, axino with the mass m_ 7 keV can decay into photon and neutrino resulting in the X-ray line signal at 3.5 , which might be the origin of unidentified X-ray emissions from galaxy clusters and Andromeda galaxy detected by the XMM-Newton X-ray observatory.
Recently, anomalous X-ray line emissions have been observed from galaxy clusters and also in the Andromeda galaxy .
We studied this decaying axino warm dark matter in the light of the recent observation of X-ray line emission from the center of galaxy clusters and Andromeda galaxy observed by XMM-Newton.

sources.summary: The paper models M31's galactic habitable zone with radial gas flows, finding a maximum at 16 kpc and an enhancement relative to the classical model.

quotes.quote: We apply detailed chemical evolution models including radial gas flows to study the galactic habitable zones in our Galaxy and M31.

For both the Milky Way and Andromeda, the main effect of the gas radial inflows is to enhance the number of stars hosting a habitable planet with respect to the ``classical'' model results, in the region of maximum probability for this occurrence, relative to the classical model results.

For Andromeda we find that the maximum number of stars with habitable planets is at 16 kpc from the center and that in the case of radial flows this number is larger by 10 relative to the stars predicted by the classical model.

sources.summary: The paper finds Andromeda's dark matter halo is generally prolate across density models and remains prolate after accounting for possible sample and tracer effects in axisymmetric modeling.

quotes.quote: It is clear from Column 3 in Table 3 and Figures 3 and 4 that the best-fit shapes of the dark matter halo in M31 are generally prolate with Q>1 for all of dark halo density models.

Therefore, in what follows, we evaluate dark halo parameters taking into account all the above possible factors and show that M31's dark halo is prolate even considering these effects on the best-fit Q value.
We have shown that our best-fit models for M31 reveal a prolate dark halo under the assumption of axisymmetry.

sources.summary: The paper cites M31 as a nearby system whose satellites form a thin, co-rotating, highly anisotropic plane, raising whether M31 is anomalous, shaped by special satellite accretion, or an outlier within CDM.

quotes.quote: For example, in our Milky Way Galaxy and neighboring M31, the satellites are found to be highly anisotropic that most of them are distributed in a great thin plane with a common rotation (e.g., Kroupa et al. 2005; Ibata et al. 2013). This raises questions if our Milky Way or M31 is anomaly or the formation and accretion of its satellites is along some special direction, or even the Cold Dark Matter theory is wrong (e.g., Kang et al. 2005; Pawlowski et al. 2014). It is highly possible that the Milky Way or M31 could be an outlier, thus its unique may not violate the general predictions from CDM model.

sources.summary: The paper models M31 as a dark-matter decay target, comparing its reported 3.5 keV X-ray line detection with Galactic-center and high-latitude constraints.

quotes.quote: Accepted *** Received ***; in original form *** firstpage - lastpage 2014 Recent studies of M31, the Galactic centre, and galaxy clusters have made tentative detections of an X-ray line at 3.5 keV that could be produced by decaying dark matter. We use high resolution simulations of the Aquarius project to predict the likely amplitude of the X-ray decay flux observed in the GC relative to that observed in M31, and also of the GC relative to other parts of the Milky Way halo and to dwarf spheroidal galaxies. We show that the reported detections from M31 and Andromeda are compatible with each other, and with upper limits arising from high galactic latitude observations, and imply a decay time 10^ 28 seconds.

sources.summary: The review cites M31 as evidence for non-axisymmetric bulges, noting disc-bulge misalignment and later hydrodynamical modelling that matched its observed properties with a triaxial bulge.

quotes.quote: He showed a misalignment between the major axes of the disc and bulge in M31, realising that this would be impossible if both the disc and bulge were oblate. This photometric misalignment is similar to the isophote twist observed in elliptical galaxies and used as an indication of triaxiality in these systems williamsschwarzchild79 .

Based on these considerations, and building on the early work of lindblad56 , berman01 demonstrated the presence of a triaxial bulge in the Andromeda galaxy (M31) by using a hydrodynamical simulation to match the observed properties of the galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper builds an M31 grand rotation curve, decomposes it into bulge, disk, and NFW halo components, and finds M31's dark halo mass about twice the Milky Way's.

quotes.quote: Grand rotation curves (GRC) within 400 kpc of M31 and the Milky Way were constructed by combining disk rotation velocities and radial velocities of satellite galaxies and globular clusters.
Comparison of the best-fit parameters revealed similarity of the disks and bulges of the two galaxies, whereas the dark halo mass of M31 was found to be twice the Galaxy's.

Rotation curve of the Andromeda galaxy M31 (NGC 224) has been obtained in details and are used for determining the mass distribution in the disk and dark halo (Sofue et al. 1999; Carignan et al. 2006; Chemin et al. 2009; Corbelli et al. 2011).

sources.summary: The paper treats M31 as an extended dark-matter target for HAWC, applies a substructure boost to its predicted annihilation flux, and finds M31 gives HAWC's strongest TeV-mass annihilation limits.

quotes.quote: In this model, the dark matter flux is ``boosted'' by 15 for the M31 galaxy and by 35 for the Virgo cluster due to these subhalos.
In figure HAWCM31 , we show the HAWC sensitivity to dark matter annihilation and decay in the M31 galaxy.
For TeV-mass dark matter annihilation, the limits from M31 should give HAWC's strongest limits.

sources.summary: The review presents M 31 as the prototype axisymmetric spiral disk-field galaxy, with a disk/central field reversal and no detected radio halo.

quotes.quote: The Andromeda galaxy M 31 (Fig. fig:m31 ) is the prototype of a dynamo-generated axisymmetric spiral disk field, with a striking sinusoidal RM variation along the star-forming ring (Fig. fig:m31rm ), which is a strong indication of an axisymmetric spiral field (mode m = 0 ) fletcher04 .

A large-scale field reversal exists between the central region and the disk in M 31 giess14 and in IC 342 beck15 . A large-scale field reversal at about constant azimuthal angle across the whole galaxy disk was found in NGC 4414 soida02 . To fulfil the divergence-free condition, this reversal cannot occur in the same volume, but e.g. between disk and halo. Indication for a large-scale field reversal between disk and halo was also found in M 51 berkhuijsen97,fletcher11,mao15 .
No radio halo was found in M 31 berkhuijsen13 and in NGC 7462 (Heesen et al., MNRAS, in press). These three galaxies have a star-formation rate that is probably too low to drive outflows.

sources.summary: The paper describes M 31 as a nearby spiral with a dense star-forming ring, using VERITAS and Fermi-LAT data to test SFR-related VHE emission and morphology.

quotes.quote: We further test the correlation between SFR and VHE emission with a deep exposure on the nearby galaxy M 31. M 31 is the closest spiral galaxy to the Milky Way and contains a dense, star-forming ring which is of particular interest to test this correlation. In , we show a detailed analysis of the detection and morphology of M 31 at lower energies with Fermi LAT data and upper limits of the extended object with VERITAS data.

sources.summary: The paper describes M31 as Andromeda, the most luminous Local Group galaxy, and gives its coordinates, distance, spiral type, CCSN rate, and lack of observed CCSNe.

quotes.quote: The M31 galaxy, also referred to as Andromeda, is the most luminous galaxy in the Local Group. Located at RA 0^ h 42^ m 44.4^ s and Dec 41^ 16^ ' 8.6^ " , M31 is a spiral galaxy located 0.77 Mpc from Earth , and is estimated to have a CCSN rate of 2.1 10^ 3 CCSNe yr ^ 1 . No CCSNe have yet been observed from M31.

sources.summary: The paper treats M31 as a best-studied gamma-ray star-forming galaxy, estimates its cosmic-ray energy density, and finds equipartition in its disk.

quotes.quote: Star-Forming Galaxies: Milky Way, M31, and LMC Aside from M82 and NGC 253, the best-studied star-forming galaxies detected in rays include the Milky Way, M31, and the Large Magellanic Cloud.
For M31 and the LMC, we find similar values for U_ CR of 1.52 and 0.58 eV cm ^ 3 , respectively.
In comparing our results for U_ CR and U_ B , we naturally find that equipartition holds for the disks of the normal star-forming galaxies tested: the Milky Way, M31, and the LMC.

sources.summary: The chapter describes M31 as a benchmark Local Group galaxy whose resolved halo surveys detect the Giant Stellar Stream and other faint substructures down to extremely low surface brightness.

quotes.quote: Likewise, in M 31 Andromeda (M31) galaxy , photometric selection of metal-poor RGB candidates removes much of the contaminating background, and has allowed detections of features as faint as 30 mag arcsec ^ 2 , including the ``giant stream'' Giant Stellar Stream (GSS) ibata2001 and other low surface brightness surface brightness features (e.g., ferguson2002 ; see Chapter 8 for more about M31). When individual stars can be resolved and spectroscopically vetted in M 31, the measurement of surface brightnesses as faint as 32 mag arcsec ^ 2 is possible (see, e.g., gilbert2012 and other results from the SPLASH survey SPLASH survey ). Thus, we are able to probe both the Milky Way and M 31 to depths of 32 mag arcsec ^ 2 , which is deep enough to sample most of the simulated halo substructures seen in simulations (discussed further in Section gx_formation_section ).

sources.summary: The paper uses M 31 as a cold, quiescent dust-map case where the rising CMB most dramatically makes the galaxy fade into the background.

quotes.quote: We exploit NGC 628, M 33, and M 31, for which there are high-quality T_ dust maps over most of their extent and whose levels of star-formation activity range from the vigorous, in NGC 628 and M 33 ( _ SFR 10^ 3 10^ 2 \ - ), to the quiescent M 31 ( _ SFR 10^ 4 10^ 3 \ - ).

The M 31 data were observed in the HELGA project The Herschel Exploitation of Local Galaxy Andromeda: http://www.astro.cardiff.ac.uk/research/astro/egalactic/surveys/?page=HELGA (P.I. Fritz; observation ID 1342211294).
In the case of the globally cold M 31, the effect of a rising CMB is most dramatic, with the entire galaxy `fading' into the CMB.

sources.summary: The paper discusses M31 as a comparison source galaxy for nanolensing, noting its larger available source-star count but stronger blending because it is not face-on.

quotes.quote: Although M33 is more compact and less massive than M31, the flux contribution from faint unresolved stars per pixel is expected to be smaller than M31 as M33 is a typical face-on galaxy with much diffuse spiral arm where bright A- and F-type main sequence stars are relatively abundant.
As a source galaxy, we have considered M33 as it has a relatively large number of blue main sequence stars and much diffuse spiral arms in comparison with M31.

However, for the purpose of detecting nanolensing events, M31 would be much suitable as the number of available source stars is larger than M33 though the effect of blending due to neighbouring stars may be stronger as it is not face-on.

sources.summary: The paper treats M31 as a massive-classical-bulge galaxy with a massive metal-rich stellar halo, consistent with but not requiring a star-rich minor or major merger origin.

quotes.quote: We add stellar halo masses and metallicities at 30 kpc along the minor axis for the MW bh16,sesar11,xue15 , M31 ibata14,gilbert14 and NGC 3115 peacock15 . peacock15 estimated a mass in low metallicity stars in NGC 3115 of 1.5 10^ 10 M_ , which they adopted as an estimate of the stellar halo mass. As there is a considerable population of high metallicity stars at large radii in NGC 3115 which could be argued to legitimately belong to its stellar halo, we choose to adopt 1.5 10^ 10 M_ as a lower limit to its halo mass. No published estimate of stellar halo mass exists for Cen A; we adopt the metallicity along the minor axis at 30 kpc from rejkuba14 .
Three galaxies with classical bulges (M31, NGC 3115, Cen A) indeed have massive metal-rich stellar halos --- carrying >20 of the total galaxy stellar mass --- indicative of a minor or major merger.

Three out of five local galaxies (M31, NGC 3115 and Cen A) with massive classical bulges have massive stellar halos M_ halo > 2 10^ 10 M_ consistent with (but not requiring) a star-rich minor or major merger origin.

sources.summary: The cosmology review describes Andromeda as having a negative redshift and as the galaxy whose Cepheids let Hubble establish it lay outside the Milky Way.

quotes.quote: Em 1923, uma tabela com 41 medidas, das quais 36 com redshifts positivos ( z 0.006 ) e apenas 5 com valores negativos, incluindo Andromeda (M31) com z=-0.001 , foi publicada no prestigiado livro de relatividade geral A tabela solicitada por Eddington foi preparada por Slipher e incluia seus últimos dados. Alguns não haviam sido publicados. do astrofísico inglês Arthur Eddington (1882-1944) .

Ainda em 1923, Edwin Hubble (1889-1953), utilizando o telescópio Hooker (ver Figura 4 ), descobriu estrelas variáveis Cefeídas em Andrômeda. Usando essas estrelas como vela-padrão As pulsantes Cefeídas foram calibradas por Henrietta Leavitt (1868-1921) permitindo medir dist a ncias extragal acticas, uma possibilidade levantada por Harlow Shapley em 1920, durante o Grande Debate. Hubble determinou a dist ancia para a gal axia de Andr omeda (M31) estabelecendo que estava efetivamente fora da Via Láctea.

sources.summary: The paper describes M31 as a green-valley LIER galaxy whose central parsec lacks molecular gas near the supermassive black hole despite expected stellar feedback.

quotes.quote: galaxies: individual: M31; galaxies: kinematics and dynamics; submillimeter: ISM; molecular data Andromeda is a galaxy, which lies in the green valley 2011ApJ...736...84M,2011A&A...526A.155T,2014ApJ...787...63J .
2015A&A...578A..74G discussed that the torus is disappearing in LIER: there is indeed little gas in the inner part of M31 2000MNRAS.312L..29M,2011A&A...536A..52M,2013A&A...549A..27M .
For M31, a SFR of 0.13 M_ yr^ 1 is expected in the sphere of influence ( R_ SOI =14 pc =3.7" ) of its supermassive black hole.

sources.summary: The paper describes Andromeda as a Local Group galaxy with a thin satellite plane whose line-of-sight velocities suggest coherent motion, though less certainly than Milky Way proper motions.

quotes.quote: The Milky Way and Andromeda galaxy are each surrounded by a thin plane of satellite galaxies that may be corotating.

This is clear for the Milky Way where accurate proper motions are available for several satellites, but it remains more uncertain for Andromeda because only velocities projected along the line of sight (LoS) are measurable.

Comparable structures have been discovered in the Milky Way halo, where the majority of the 11 classical satellites share a coherent orbital motion (established with proper motion measurements of individual stars from the satellites) , and for the Andromeda galaxy, for which 13 out of 15 satellites follow a coherent LoS velocity trend .

sources.summary: The review reports a marginal AME detection in M31's integrated spectrum and notes high-resolution microwave observations with the Sardinia Radio Telescope.

quotes.quote: found a marginal detection of AME from the integrated spectrum of the Andromeda galaxy (M31), at an amplitude of 0.7 0.3 Jy ( 2.3 ). The amplitude is in line with expectations from the emissivity found in our Galaxy. M31 is also the subject of high resolution microwave observations performed with the recently commissioned 64-m Sardinia Radio Telescope SRT; Prandoni2017 ; detailed C-band (5.7--7.7 GHz) intensity, spectroscopic, and polarization observations have been conducted over 7 deg ^2 around M31 (Battistelli Fatigoni, in prep.) and K-band (18--26.5 GHz) observations are expected in 2019.

sources.summary: The paper says M31 has unusually high free-fall star formation efficiency not explained by metallicity corrections, possibly reflecting beam-filling bias from small, sparse, tenuous clouds.

quotes.quote: M31 shows a higher \ that can not be explained by the metallicity-dependent \ only. This apparent high efficiency may partially reflect beam-filling effects. M31 has a low molecular-to-atomic gas fraction, and if the clouds are small, widely spaced, and tenuous compared to the beam (as suggested by SUN18 ), then the long \ may be partially an observational bias due to low beam filling factor.

sources.summary: The paper uses M31 galaxy parameters to estimate mirror magnetic-field evolution, compares the modeled field with M33, and argues mirror fields matter inside about 5 kpc.

quotes.quote: As examples, for M33 galaxy by substituting v'=120 km s ^ 1 , M'=6 10^ 10 M_ , R_ g0 =17 kpc , we obtain a_0/a_*=10.1 , _*'=3.5 10^ 16 s ^ 1 , t_*=1.7 10^ 16 s . For M31 galaxy v'=190 km s ^ 1 , M'=1.2 10^ 12 M_ , R_ g0 =35 kpc and we get the following values: a_0/a_*=7.0, _*'=7.5 10^ 16 s ^ 1 , t_*=3.0 10^ 16 s . The dependence of the mirror magnetic field on parameter x for M33 and M31 galaxies at the moment of the inhomogeneity separation is shown in figure graph2 .
It is clear that the mirror magnetic field becomes significant at small radii where u' v'_ orb . According to rotation curves for M31 and M33 this happens at radii less than 5 kpc.

sources.summary: The FOBOS science case treats M31/Andromeda as a Local Group assembly target, emphasizing halo substructure and giant-star spectroscopy calibrated against simulations of M31's formation history.

quotes.quote: Studies of individual stars in the Milky Way (MW), Magellanic Clouds, Andromeda (M31), Triangulum galaxy (M33), and numerous dwarf satellites provide an exquisitely detailed look at specific examples of galaxy assembly and evolution.

Performing a similar analysis on the M31 halo is highly desireable but more challenging because individual main-sequence stars at the distance of M31 are too faint for 10m telescopes. Thus spectroscopic training efforts must focus on giant stars in the M31 halo and be calibrated with hydrodynamical simulations that account for M31's differing formation history e.g. 2005MNRAS.356.1071R,li19 .

sources.summary: The paper uses M 31 as a key nearby halo example, noting its intermediate-age, metal-rich halo, satellites and streams, and merger-driven structural complexity.

quotes.quote: By way of example, deep Hubble Space Telescope (HST) imaging on the minor axis of M 31 ( Brown03 ; Brown06 ) revealed a substantial intermediate-age population. The halo of M 31 is mostly metal rich ( Rich96 ; durrell01 ; Bellazzini03 ) while also hosting both globular clusters, RR Lyrae stars, and a host of substructures in the form of satellites and streams (e.g. Mcconnachie09 ). Simulations like those of e.g. Mori08 show that a minor merger with a galaxy of mass M 10^9 M_ can reproduce the many complex structures similar to those seen in the M 31 halo as well as ejecting stars from the disk.

sources.summary: M 31 is described as a spiral with a prominent classical bulge, a large combined bulge-plus-stellar-halo fraction, and a bulge/halo likely produced by significant mergers.

quotes.quote: The Andromeda Nebula M 31 and the spiral M 81 have relatively prominent classical bulges.
The galaxies M 31 and M 81 have large bulge plus stellar halo fractions, (B+H)/T = 0.46 and 0.36.
The usual thinking is that the large bulge and stellar halo of M 31 are the results of quite significant mergers.

sources.summary: The paper uses HAWC gamma-ray flux measurements of the Andromeda galaxy as a dark-matter decay target to constrain sterile-neutrino lifetimes at roughly 7 × 10^24 s.

quotes.quote: To compute the constraints from HAWC data, we use the differential fluxes provided in Ref.  . In that study, the HAWC collaboration measured the gamma-ray fluxed from the Andromeda galaxy, an ideal target to look for dark matter decay debris. They measured fluxes of photons with energies ranging from a TeV to 100 TeV and were able to place constraints on E^2 N _ / E_ 10^ 12 10^ 11 \ TeV \ cm ^ 2 \ s ^ 1 . Using these results, we find a constraint of the sterile neutrino lifetime of roughly 7 10^ 24 \ s (see Fig. ( fig:constrants )).

sources.summary: The paper treats Andromeda as an X-ray target whose Chandra and NuSTAR observations constrain keV dark-matter decay, noting those observations provide some of the tightest limits.

quotes.quote: In parallel, the observations of Andromeda (M31) galaxy by Chandra and NuSTAR , Milky Way (MW) galaxy by Chandra , Galactic Center (GC) by NuSTAR and diffuse X-ray background by XMM and HEAO-1 also constrain the parameter space of keV DM .

The upper limits from the observations of diffuse X-ray background by XMM (at 95 C.L.) , Andromeda (M31) galaxy by Chandra (at 95 C.L.) and NuSTAR (at 95 C.L.) , diffuse cosmic hard X-Ray by HEAO-1 (at 95 C.L.) , Milky Way (MW) galaxy by Chandra (at 99 C.L.) , Galactic Center (GC) by NuSTAR (at 95 C.L.) are shown by green, cyan, brown, gray, black and blue solid lines, respectively.

As mentioned earlier, the Chandra observations of Andromeda and Milky Way (MW) galaxies as well as the NuSTAR observations of Andromeda (M31) and Galactic Center (GC) put the tightest constraints in the m_ plane.

sources.summary: The paper discusses Andromeda/M31 halo emission, debated bubble-pair interpretations, its hot gaseous CGM, and future observational searches for predicted X-ray features.

quotes.quote: Now, evidence for extended ray emission from the halo of Andromeda has been discussed and confirmed in the literature Pshirkov.2016, Ackermann.2017 : however, whether this can be associated with bubble pairs, i.e. quasi-spherical features symmetrically located perpendicular to the M31 galactic disk remains debated Karwin.2019 . On the X-ray emission side, it has been argued that a substantial mass of the CGM in M31 should be present in its hot gaseous diffuse atmosphere Lehner.2015, Bregman.2018 : however, whether X-ray features in the halo of Andromeda as the ones predicted here could be detected with future telescopes and observations remains to be assessed. Certainly, our findings motivate further efforts, both theoretical and observational, in the direction of our neighboring M31, and more generally in the local Universe -- as we outline below.

sources.summary: The paper uses the Andromeda galaxy (M31) as a dark-matter-neutrino target, adopts an Einasto dark-matter distribution, and finds its decay sensitivities competitive with leading experiments.

quotes.quote: In this analysis, we use galaxy clusters, the Andromeda galaxy (M31), and satellite dwarf galaxies of the Milky Way as targets.
The model for the dark matter distribution in the Andromeda galaxy is adopted from and is based on the Einasto profile .
For the Virgo cluster and the Andromeda galaxy, the sensitivities are competitive with other leading experiments.

sources.summary: The paper describes M31 as a metal-rich Local Group galaxy whose central H II regions and inner-disk stars favor Z=0.02, with some stellar abundances reaching O/H+12=9.0.

quotes.quote: Within the Local Group, the present day metallicity of M31 is considered to be highly supersolar based on strong-line H ii region calibrations Zaritsky1994 , such that Z = 0.03 is commonly adopted. However, more recent direct H ii determinations infer a central metallicity of O/H + 12 = 8.7 to 8.9 Zurita2012 , with similar abundances from early-type stars in the inner disk Venn2000, Smartt2001 , such that Z = 0.02 is more suitable to M31. Stellar abundances as high as O/H + 12 = 9.0 have been obtained Trundle2002 , potentially attributable to azimuthal variations.

sources.summary: The paper uses M31 as a massive disk-galaxy model, fitting its rotation curve with a lognormal density distribution and comparing its velocity-curve shape to the Milky Way.

quotes.quote: In this paper, we build a density model for the disk of a massive galaxy using M31 as a model, and use numerical analysis to investigate how large random motions---such as those arising from close interactions---might be rapidly damped to small random oscillations about their circular trajectory of 20-30 km sec ^ 1 within the timescale of orbital rotation, and these mechanisms may be sufficient to stabilize the orbits against random gravitational fluctuations while retaining the stability demanded by Toomre .

The LN model provides a curve consistent with the original observations of M31 by Rubin and Ford , Carignan et al , and Corbelli et al , and gives a convenient method of generating () for numerical stability analysis (Figure fig:M31 ). Furthermore, the general shape of the velocity curve for M31 is broadly similar to recent curves for the Milky Way Galaxy (Figure 1).

sources.summary: The paper identifies Andromeda (M31) as a nearby spiral galaxy within the Telescope Array excess region and a potential UHECR source candidate for nitrogen or silicon scenarios.

quotes.quote: As shown in fig:distance , Andromeda (M31) lies at a distance of 752 27 kpc Riess_2012 and within the contour region of the new excess of TA. Our neighboring galaxy is therefore a potential local UHECR source candidate for intermediate-mass isotopes like nitrogen and silicon. However, note that being a spiral galaxy, this seems to be a bold claim as the question of the acceleration sites of such energetic cosmic rays cannot be easily addressed in this case.

sources.summary: The paper treats M31's motion relative to the Milky Way as central to Local Group mass estimates, citing new proper-motion results and deriving simulated tangential and radial velocity ranges.

quotes.quote: The kinematics of the Milky Way (MW) and M31, the dominant galaxies in the Local Group (LG), can be used to estimate the LG total mass. New results on the M31 proper motion have recently been used to improve that estimate.
Using a large enough simulation, we find M31 tangential and radial velocities relative to the MW to be in the range v_ tan =105^ +94 _ 59 and v_ rad =-108^ +68 _ 81 , respectively.

sources.summary: The simulation paper uses Andromeda as an observed comparison point, noting its extended disk, massive bulge and SMBH, rich halo and satellites, high stellar mass, and average disk size for its mass.

quotes.quote: The Galaxy and Andromeda themselves present different observed features: Andromeda exhibits a thicker and more extended disk, a larger and more massive bulge, a more massive central SMBH, a younger and more metal-rich stellar halo population, more numerous tidal streams and satellites, more numerous and more massive globular clusters, and overall a larger total stellar mass and lower SFR than the Milky Way -- see next sections and figures.

In fact, for Andromeda, stellar mass estimates (evaluated either within 30 kpc or by summing up the stellar mass of bulge and disk) vary between 7.8 10^ 10 and 1.5 10^ 11 , i.e. approximately in the 10^ 10.9-11.2 range Geehan.2006, Barmby.2006, Chemin.2009,Tamm.2012, Sick.2015 .

For the case of the Galaxy and assuming that the TNG50 stellar disk lengths are well consistent with those of the real galaxy populations both in the median and in the galaxy-to-galaxy variation see e.g. for additional comparisons of TNG50 galaxy sizes to data Pillepich.2019 , the top of Fig. fig:stellardisks indicates that the Milky Way has a rather compact stellar disk given its mass, as it settles at the lower end of the TNG50 distribution, while Andromeda appears a rather average galaxy for its mass.

sources.summary: The paper cites M31/Andromeda as having anisotropically distributed satellites, including the Great Plane of Andromeda, and a second roughly perpendicular positional plane.

quotes.quote: The discovery that satellites orbiting around the Milky Way (MW) and Andromeda (M31) have a highly anisotropic distribution has been long considered to be one of the most-challenging issues for CDM see for a review Bullock17-review,Pawlowski18 .

Satellites orbiting around M31 have also been discovered to be anisotropically distributed around it Koch06,McConnachie06,Metz07 , with almost half the population (15 out of 27 satellites) forming a thin plane in positions, referred to as the ``GPoA'' or ``Great Plane of Andromeda'', and almost edge-on from our perspective. Moreover, in Paper I it has been shown that a second positional plane, roughly perpendicular to the former, shows up in M31.

sources.summary: The paper describes M31 as part of the Local Group's MW-M31 pair, with satellites between the pair, strong same-hemisphere satellite lopsidedness, and a possible dwarf-association origin for that asymmetry.

quotes.quote: In the Local Group, the Milky Way and M31 are considered as a galaxy pair, and there are 25 satellites located in the area between them.
Examining isolated systems such as the Milky Way (MW) and M31, it has been observed that 7 of the 11 brightest MW satellites and 21 of the 27 M31 satellites are located in the same hemisphere.
The physical source of LSD has been explored, with suggesting that the lopsidedness of M31 is more likely due to a dwarf association falling into its halo than the tidal field of the Milky Way.

sources.summary: The paper models M31's nuclear disk as a long-lived m=1 mode whose morphology and kinematics reproduce the observed double-nucleus asymmetries and require a central mass concentration.

quotes.quote: The nearly keplerian nuclear disk of M31 is subject to a natural m=1 mode, with a very slow pattern speed (3 km/s/pc for M_ BH = 7 10^7 ), that can be maintained during more than a thousand dynamical times. The resulting morphology and kinematics of the mode can reproduce the M 31 nuclear-disk photometry and mean stellar velocity, including the observed asymmetries. It requires a central mass concentration and a cold disk system representing between 20 and 40 of its mass.

sources.summary: The paper treats M31 as the nearby spiral target whose X-ray output is dominated by point sources, with little current star formation and an old stellar core.

quotes.quote: At 760 kpc ( ) the Andromeda Galaxy ( M31 ) is the nearest spiral galaxy to our own. The X-ray emission of M31 is dominated by point sources, either X-ray binaries or supernova remnants with luminosities 10^ 35 erg s^ 1 .
There is little star formation in M31 and the core is dominated by old stars.

sources.summary: The paper uses M31 carbon-star number ratios to trace a radial abundance profile, reporting agreement with metallicity gradients from HII regions and supernova remnants.

quotes.quote: Indeed Brewer et al. (1995) have imaged five fields along the semi-major axis of M31 to derive the radial profile of N _ C /N _ M . Using the compilation of Pritchet et al. (1987) to calibrate the N _ C /N _ M vs. [ Fe/H ] relation, and the observed N _ C /N _ M radial profile, they have derived the radial abundance profile along the semi-major axis of M31. They find rather reasonable agreement with the metallicity gradient observed by Blair et al. (1981, 1982) and Dennefeld Kunth (1981) using HII regions and supernova remnants.

sources.summary: The paper argues that M31's hot galaxy halo could produce one of the sky's strongest integrated SZ signals, making its baryonic halo gas directly testable.

quotes.quote: While massive clusters produce the strongest central SZ decrements, we point out that a local galaxy halo, specifically the halo of M31, may be one of the brightest integrated SZ sources in the sky. For various realistic gas distributions consistent with current X-ray limits, we show that the integrated SZ decrement from M31 will be comparable to decrements already detected in more distant sources, provided its halo contains an appreciable quantity of hot gas. A measurement of this decrement would provide direct information on the mass, spatial distribution and thermodynamic state of hot gas in a low-mass halo, and could place important constraints on current models of galaxy formation.

sources.summary: The review reports GALEX UV radial light and color profiles for M31, finding UV emission more extended than HII-region gas and a radial FUV/NUV trend comparable to other nearby disks.

quotes.quote: M31, M32 and M33: Radial Gradients For M31 and M33 (Figures 3 4, respectively) Thilker et al. have determined the UV radial light and color profiles and compared them with the distribution of ionized gas as traced by H emission. It is found that the extent of the UV emission, in both targets, is greater than the extent of the observed HII regions and diffuse ionized gas.
The radial profiles in FUV/NUV for other galaxies (M31, M33 and M83) show the same trend; however, the magnitude of the effect is variable from galaxy to galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper describes M 31 as radio-faint but magnetically extended, with a regular field reaching at least 25 kpc and a strongly dominant axisymmetric magnetic mode.

quotes.quote: Radio-faint galaxies like M 31 and M 33 have B_ t 6 G, while 15 G is typical for grand-design galaxies like M 51, M 83 and NGC 6946.
Han et al. \ ( ) found evidence that the regular field in M 31 extends to at least 25 kpc radius without significant decrease in strength.

M 31 hosts a strongly dominating axisymmetric field (Berkhuijsen et al. \ ), extending to at least 25 kpc radius (Han et al. \ ) and at least 1 kpc height above the galaxy's plane (Fletcher et al. \ ).

sources.summary: The paper says M31 searches have not detected water masers, notes major star-forming regions were targeted, and predicts future telescopes could find about 17 faint masers.

quotes.quote: Searches toward M31 and other Local Group galaxies have not yet yielded a detection.

Since the largest star forming regions were targeted in the maser searches at least in M33, M31, IC 1613, and NGC 6822 it is not likely that additional masers exist above the detection limits mentioned in Table  galaxies .

Based on this analysis one can expect to find 17 and 5 water masers with flux densities above 1 mJy in M31 and NGC 6822, respectively, that could be used to measure the proper motions and geometric distances of these galaxies with future radio telescopes like the Square Kilometre Array (SKA).

sources.summary: The review identifies M31 as the largest spiral GCS study, noting rotation in both metal-poor and metal-rich clusters, a controversial thin-disk GCS, and possible remnant substructure.

quotes.quote: By far the largest study is of the M31 GCS, resulting in 321 velocities, largely from the MMT and the WHT .
In M31, there is rotation in both the metal-poor and metal-rich GCs, a thin-disk GCS whose age is controversial, and again a metal-poor stellar halo with a decreasing dispersion profile .
Orbit analyses can also find remnant substructures from accretion and interaction events, as may have already been identified in M31 and M49 .

sources.summary: The paper treats M31 as a major Local Group galaxy whose poorly known proper and tangential motion affects reconstructions of Local Group dynamics and satellite evolution.

quotes.quote: Since the first observations of 1917AJ.....30..175B , perhaps the most important steps toward understanding the nature of the LG is the determination of the proper motion and tangential motion of M31, which unfortunately are still poorly known. The first important study dealing with the evolution of the LG, supposedly in isolation , is by 1959ApJ...130..705K . The authors based their study on the spherical gravitational collapse and expansion approximation. As consequence of it, with the aid of the third Kepler's law they predicted only radial orbits for the MW and M31 from the origins to the present time. The possibility of a tangential motion for M31 with respect to the MW was automatically ruled out.

sources.summary: The paper compares chemical evolution models with M31 bulge metallicity data and concludes that a flatter IMF than the universal IMF better fits galactic bulges.

quotes.quote: Finally, Fig. 4 shows the MDs resulting from our reference model and the model with the UIMF compared to the MD of M31 as measured by Sarajedini Jablonka (2005) translating the observed color-magnitude diagram at 1.6 kpc (G170 bulge field) from the center into a MD function by means of red giant branches with various metallicities. This MD, though still consistent with the ones of the Galactic bulge and therefore indicating a similar enrichment history of the two bulges, is slightly more metal-poor on average and is compatible with both the reference model and the model with the UIMF; however, we also plotted the MD calculated with x_2=1.1 , like in Matteucci Brocato (1990, model MB90), which gives the best fit. In any case, we can safely conclude that an IMF with x 1 is more suitable for galactic bulges than the UIMF.
